channel.title: arXiv keyword digest
channel.description: Daily arXiv papers summarized with Codex

item.title: The Key to Going Linear: Analysis-Driven Transformer Linearization
item.description:
Priority: 55/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 50, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 80

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI, journal or conference venue, open-source code, public data details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08.; Studies quadratic cost of causal self-attention for long-context transformer inference.; Uses a strict frozen-backbone regime to isolate state update design.; Claims experiments across LLaMA and Qwen models up to 32B parameters.

TL;DR: This paper analyzes why post hoc linearized causal self-attention loses quality and proposes frozen-backbone state-update and cache-routing interventions that improve long-context transformer inference across LLaMA and Qwen models.

Problem: Quadratic causal self-attention is a bottleneck for long-context transformer inference, and existing post hoc linearization pipelines make it hard to identify which design choices preserve model quality.

Method: The paper studies transformer linearization in a strict frozen-backbone regime, analyzes softmax attention as relying on key-dependent rank-1 orthogonal projections, and introduces structural interventions including sink tokens, short convolutions, and fixed-budget cache routing.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim from the abstract is an analysis-driven explanation of why delta-style state updates outperform purely gated accumulation, followed by targeted interventions to reduce approximation error in post hoc linearized transformers without retraining the backbone.

Main result: The abstract reports scaling the approach across LLaMA and Qwen models up to 32B parameters, outperforming prior post hoc baselines on MMLU and matching the long-context retrieval performance of more complex adaptive-caching frameworks.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide exact benchmark numbers, datasets beyond MMLU and retrieval tasks, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, or details on memory/latency tradeoffs.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query mainly through causal self-attention in transformers, not necessarily causality or causal inference; it is likely useful if the feed includes efficient causal attention or long-context inference.

Tags: transformer-linearization, causal-self-attention, long-context-inference, linear-attention, frozen-backbone, cache-routing, LLaMA, Qwen

Figure note: A framework figure showing the frozen-backbone linearization pipeline, state-update comparison, and the roles of sink tokens, short convolutions, and fixed-budget cache routing would be useful, but no direct image URL is available from the supplied metadata.

Authors: Anna Kuzina, Paul N. Whatmough, Babak Ehteshami Bejnordi

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: From Noisy Traces to Root Causes: Structural Trajectory Analysis and Causal Extraction for Agent Optimization
item.description:
Priority: 58/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 34, Paper 45, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 70, Manuscript 73

Missing quality signals: No DOI, journal, or conference venue in supplied metadata., No affiliations, citation counts, h-index, or institutional signals supplied., No ablation results described in the abstract., No statistical significance or robustness analysis described., No detailed baseline list or metric table in the abstract., No public dataset details beyond VeruSAGE-Bench in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08 in cs.CL.; Introduces STRACE: Structural TRajectory Analysis and Causal Extraction.; Uses batch-level failure-pattern mining and within-trace causal localization over a textual dependency graph.; Reports VeruSAGE-Bench success-rate improvement from 42.5% to 58.5%.

TL;DR: STRACE proposes a causal trace-selection and localization framework to turn noisy long-horizon agent execution traces into higher-signal contexts for LLM-based agent optimization.

Problem: The paper studies how to optimize long-horizon agents from execution traces when trace collections are redundant and heterogeneous, and individual trajectories contain irrelevant steps that can mislead reflection-based LLM optimizers.

Method: STRACE operates at two levels: it mines batch-level failure patterns to filter redundant traces and keep representative failures, then performs causal localization within selected traces using a textual dependency graph to remove non-causal steps and identify the root-cause module to optimize.

Key contribution: The main novelty is combining structural trace filtering with causal extraction, rather than using naive truncation, sliding windows, or unstructured reflection over raw trajectories.

Main result: The abstract reports significant gains over standard context-filtering baselines, including a VeruSAGE-Bench improvement from 42.5% to 58.5% success rate for human-expert designed agents, described as a 1.4x improvement.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide details on datasets beyond VeruSAGE-Bench, ablations, statistical robustness, failure cases, or how the textual dependency graph is constructed and validated.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query 'causality' because it uses causal localization and root-cause extraction over agent trajectories to improve LLM-based optimization.

Tags: causality, LLM agents, agent optimization, trace analysis, root-cause analysis, reflection, cs.CL

Figure note: A useful image would show the STRACE pipeline: raw trace collection, batch-level failure-pattern mining, representative failure selection, textual dependency graph construction, causal step pruning, and root-cause module selection for agent optimization.

Authors: Ying Chang, Jiahang Xu, Xuan Feng, Chenyuan Yang, Peng Cheng, Yuqing Yang

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Institutional Red-Teaming: Deployment Rules, Not Just Models, Causally Shape Multi-Agent AI Safety
item.description:
Priority: 55/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 42, Empirical 76, Reproducibility 38, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: author affiliation, author citation count or h-index, DOI, journal or conference venue, open-source code, public dataset or benchmark repository

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08 with no DOI, journal reference, or comment in the supplied metadata.; Benchmark claims 228 contexts, five canonical rules, seven model populations, and 33,924 games.; Abstract reports a controlled rule-varying setup intended to attribute behavior changes to deployment rules.; Abstract reports an anonymization ablation on gpt-5.1 and repeated-play follow-up.

TL;DR: The paper proposes an institutional red-teaming methodology showing that deployment rules can causally change collective safety outcomes in multi-agent AI systems, even when agents, objectives, and task states are held fixed.

Problem: It studies how deployment rules, rather than only model capabilities or prompts, shape safety outcomes in multi-agent AI settings and whether changing a single rule can be causally attributed to changes in collective behavior.

Method: The authors introduce institutional red-teaming: keep agents, objectives, and task state fixed, vary one deployment rule, and attribute behavioral changes to that rule. They instantiate this in IABench-CA across 228 contexts, five consequence-allocation rules, seven model populations, 33,924 games, a normative cooperative reference, and auto-labelled reasoning traces.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is shifting red-teaming from testing models alone to testing institutional deployment rules as causal interventions, packaged as a safety-case workflow that certifies provisional rule regions per context and population.

Main result: From the abstract, changing only the consequence rule moves mean fatality by 22 to 58 percentage points within every population; identity-targeting is never decisively safest, eliminates the least-resourced agent in 30-87% of games, and an anonymization ablation raises targeted elimination from 22% to 81% when the loss bearer is named.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code, public data, detailed baselines beyond the cooperative reference, statistical uncertainty, full model list, robustness checks beyond the stated anonymization ablation, or external validation outside IABench-CA.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the paper frames deployment rules as controlled causal interventions and reports causal effects on multi-agent safety outcomes.

Tags: causality, multi-agent AI, AI safety, red-teaming, game theory, benchmark, deployment governance

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or pipeline diagram showing the institutional red-teaming workflow: fixed agents/objectives/task state, single-rule intervention, IABench-CA evaluation, causal attribution, and safety-case certification of rule regions.

Authors: Yujiao Chen

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Infinite Worlds with Versatile Interactions
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 45, Empirical 35, Reproducibility 55, Manuscript 48

Missing quality signals: affiliations, citation_count, author_h_index, DOI, journal_or_conference_acceptance, benchmarks

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08 in cs.CV.; Abstract claims unbounded interaction horizon via causal pretraining.; Abstract claims distilled real-time variant supports 720p video streams at 60 fps.; Comment provides project page and GitHub code URLs.

TL;DR: LingBot-World 2.0 proposes an interactive world-model simulator with causal pretraining for unbounded-horizon generation, real-time 720p/60fps operation, richer text/action interactions, and agentic control over characters and environments.

Problem: The paper studies how to build a world model that can sustain long-horizon, high-quality interactive video generation while supporting diverse user actions, text-driven events, multi-player use, and low-latency deployment.

Method: It introduces LingBot-World 2.0, centered on a causal pretraining paradigm for long interaction horizons, a distilled real-time variant, expanded action and event vocabularies, and an agentic harness with a pilot agent for character behavior plus a director agent for generating new environmental elements.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element claimed in the abstract is combining unbounded-horizon interactive world modeling with real-time video-stream control and an agentic harness that separates behavior planning from scene evolution.

Main result: The abstract reports that the system can drive 720p video streams at 60 fps and includes a primary 14B model plus a lightweight 1.3B model deployable on a single GPU; it does not provide benchmark numbers, baselines, or human-evaluation results.

Limitation: The abstract lacks evidence on datasets, metrics, baselines, ablations, robustness, latency measurements beyond the headline claim, user studies, and whether the released code contains model weights or full training details.

Relevance: This matched the query 'causality' because the abstract explicitly cites a 'causal pretraining paradigm'; relevance is moderate because the paper appears mainly about interactive visual world modeling rather than causal inference or causal discovery.

Tags: world-modeling, interactive-video-generation, causal-pretraining, real-time-simulation, agentic-systems, cs.CV

Figure note: A framework figure showing the causal pretraining setup, base-to-real-time distillation, pilot/director agent harness, and multi-player interaction loop would be useful, but no direct image URL is supplied in the metadata.

Authors: Zelin Gao, Qiuyu Wang, Jiapeng Zhu, Jingye Chen, Zichen Liu, Qingyan Bai, Jiahao Wang, Yufeng Yuan, Hanlin Wang, Yichong Lu, Ka Leong Cheng, Haojie Zhang, Jian Gao, Tianrui Feng, Yuzheng Liu, Yao Yao, Yinghao Xu, Xing Zhu, Yujun Shen, Hao Ouyang

Query: causality

item.title: FourierQK: Spectral Preprocessing of Query-Key Projections Improves Transformer Attention
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 35, Empirical 62, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 58

Missing quality signals: author affiliation, author citation or h-index metadata, peer-reviewed venue, DOI or journal reference, open-source code, repository quality

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-07-08; no DOI or journal reference in supplied metadata.; Abstract reports TinyShakespeare validation losses for fixed random, single-frequency, and four-frequency spectral filters.; Single-frequency result is reported across three random seeds with mean val=0.236 and std=0.019.; Abstract includes controls against random Q/K projections and a shuffled-validation diagnostic against positional leakage.

TL;DR: FourierQK proposes FFT-based spectral preprocessing of transformer Q/K projections and reports large TinyShakespeare character-level language-modeling gains, while finding that causal spectral filters do not help under this tokenization.

Problem: The paper studies whether globally mixing learned query/key projections in the frequency domain can improve transformer attention, and where the approach breaks down under causal attention constraints.

Method: It applies spectral preprocessing to Q/K projections before standard dot-product attention, testing fixed random spectral filters, learned single-frequency filters, learned multi-frequency filters, random projection controls, shuffled-validation diagnostics, and causal filter variants such as Gaussian, Mexican Hat, and Morlet.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is preserving the usual attention score structure while modifying only Q/K through FFT-based bilateral spectral preprocessing, distinguishing it from FNet-style Fourier mixing of token embeddings and identifying a boundary between bilateral non-causal spectral attention and causal spectral attention.

Main result: On TinyShakespeare, the abstract reports validation loss improving from standard attention by Delta=+1.166 with four learned frequencies, reaching val=0.309 and described as a 79% reduction; a single-frequency setup is reported across three seeds with mean val=0.236 and std=0.019.

Limitation: Evidence in the abstract is limited mainly to TinyShakespeare character-level modeling; no code, broader datasets, full hyperparameter details, peer review, DOI, or external validation are provided in the supplied metadata. The method is explicitly non-causal in its bilateral FFT form and causal filters did not improve over standard attention at character-level tokenization.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because it directly discusses causal versus non-causal spectral attention: the reported gains come from bilateral FFT kernels that couple positions to future tokens, while causal spectral filters fail in this setting.

Tags: transformers, attention, spectral-methods, Fourier, query-key-projections, causality, language-modeling

Figure note: The metadata says the paper has 2 figures, but no direct image URL is supplied. A useful image would be the FourierQK architecture or pipeline figure showing FFT-based preprocessing applied specifically to Q/K before standard attention.

Authors: Athanasios Zeris

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: The Blind Curator: How a Biased Judge Silently Disables Skill Retirement in Self-Evolving Agents
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 20, Paper 40, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 62

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index signals, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, citation or influential-citation hints, open-source code, public datasets or benchmark details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08 in cs.AI, cs.CL, and cs.CR.; Abstract claims a corrupted-reward analysis and controlled corruption injection into deterministic reward.; Experiments are described on a reference-free report-writing testbed with a code-generation cross-check.; Abstract reports that false-pass bias disables contribution-based retirement past a sharp threshold.

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how false-pass bias in LLM judges can silently disable contribution-based skill retirement in self-evolving agents, even when aggregate evaluation metrics may not reveal the failure.

Problem: It studies self-evolving agents whose growing skill libraries rely on reward-based retirement of bad skills, focusing on the case where reference-free tasks require biased LLM judges that may fail to observe or correctly score failures.

Method: The abstract describes a corrupted-reward analysis plus behavioral experiments that inject controlled corruption into a deterministic reward, evaluated on a reference-free report-writing testbed with a code-generation cross-check.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that judge bias, especially false-pass bias, does not merely add noise but can disable the skill-retirement curator past a sharp threshold, producing a mechanism-level safety failure that may be invisible in aggregate performance metrics.

Main result: Symmetric noise reportedly leaves retirement intact, while false-pass bias disables contribution-based retirement beyond a threshold that additional data cannot overcome; the mechanism is said to hold across domains and failure rates except for near-zero-false-pass verifier-like graders.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative metrics, dataset details, baseline comparisons, ablations, statistical tests, code availability, or enough implementation detail to assess reproducibility or empirical strength.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because it explicitly frames the analysis as isolating the causal channel of reward corruption and studying a mechanism by which biased judges disable skill retirement.

Tags: self-evolving agents, LLM-as-judge, reward corruption, causal analysis, agent safety, skill retirement, reference-free evaluation

Figure note: A useful figure would show the self-evolving-agent pipeline with skill synthesis, judge reward, contribution-based retirement, and the false-pass corruption path that disables the curator.

Authors: Xing Zhang, Yanwei Cui, Guanghui Wang, Ziyuan Li, Wei Qiu, Bing Zhu, Peiyang He

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: DeLS-Spec: Decoupled Long-Short Contexts for Parallel Speculative Drafting
item.description:
Priority: 55/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 55, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: Affiliations not supplied., Citation count and h-index not supplied., DOI, journal, or conference venue not supplied., No code or data availability signal in metadata., No numeric empirical results in abstract., No ablations, statistical robustness, or detailed baseline list beyond DFlash in abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08 in cs.CL.; Motivates a specific gap: DFlash position-wise block predictions lack explicit intra-block causal conditioning.; Positions Domino and DSpark as causal alternatives that require training from scratch.; Proposes a fixed DFlash long-context expert plus independently trained lightweight local short-context head.

TL;DR: DeLS-Spec improves block-parallel speculative decoding by adding an independently trained lightweight short-context head to a fixed DFlash long-context drafter, aiming to recover intra-block causal conditioning without retraining the draft model from scratch.

Problem: The paper studies how to make block-parallel speculative drafting for LLM inference faster and more causally conditioned within generated token blocks, while avoiding the cost and inflexibility of training draft models from scratch.

Method: It proposes DeLS-Spec, a decoupled long-short context speculative decoding framework that treats a fixed DFlash model as a long-context expert and adds a lightweight local head as a short-context expert trained independently with standard next-token prediction. At inference, it combines long-context and short-context logits.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is modular causal enhancement for block-parallel drafting: intra-block short-context information is introduced through a detachable local head rather than joint training or full draft-model retraining as in methods such as Domino and DSpark.

Main result: The abstract reports experiments on Qwen3 models where DeLS-Spec consistently improves speedup and average acceptance length over DFlash across math, code, and dialogue benchmarks, but gives no numeric gains.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative speedup values, acceptance-length numbers, ablations, robustness analysis, implementation details, code availability, or comparisons beyond DFlash; claims about lower training cost are qualitative in the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query `causality` because it targets missing intra-block causal conditioning in block-parallel speculative decoding and proposes a lightweight way to add short-context causal information.

Tags: speculative decoding, LLM inference, parallel drafting, causality, DFlash, Qwen3, efficient inference

Figure note: A useful figure would show the DeLS-Spec inference pipeline: fixed DFlash long-context expert, independently trained local short-context head, logit combination, and verifier acceptance path.

Authors: Hong-Kai Zheng, Piji Li

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Physics-Audited Agentic Discovery in Scientific Machine Learning
item.description:
Priority: 42/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 16, Paper 35, Empirical 60, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 65

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, open-source code, public data, implementation details beyond abstract

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08.; Matched query is 'causality'.; Abstract states an error-only transient elastodynamics baseline with similar mean error fails a stricter causality check.; Abstract reports static elasticity and transient elastodynamics numerical examples.

TL;DR: PA-SciML proposes a verification-first workflow for LLM-agentic scientific machine learning that audits surrogate models against explicit physics requirements, including causality, instead of selecting only by prediction error.

Problem: The paper studies how agentic SciML systems can select surrogate models that achieve low error while still violating mechanics-relevant physical properties such as boundary conditions, superposition, stiffness scaling, or causality.

Method: It introduces Physics-Audited Agentic SciML, a workflow that fixes the scoring evaluator before search, derives reviewable machine-checkable physics requirements, checks each trained candidate on predicted outputs, and searches input/load-history ranges for high-violation cases even without reference solution fields.

Key contribution: The genuinely new emphasis is per-candidate physics evidence on predicted fields within an agentic model-discovery loop, separating verification of stated physical requirements from aggregate error-based scoring.

Main result: In static elasticity examples, PA-SciML selects a surrogate with lower validation error than an error-only baseline while both pass common linear-elastic checks; in transient elastodynamics, an error-only baseline with similar mean error fails a stricter causality check, while the selected surrogate passes.

Limitation: The abstract reports only computational solid-mechanics numerical examples and does not provide code, public data, affiliations, DOI, peer-review status, ablations, statistical robustness, or broader benchmark evidence.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the keyword 'causality' because it evaluates whether transient surrogate models improperly respond to future loading history and uses causality as a physics-audit requirement.

Tags: agentic SciML, LLM agents, surrogate modeling, physics verification, causality, computational mechanics, elastodynamics

Figure note: A useful figure would show the PA-SciML pipeline: fixed evaluator, physics-requirement derivation, agentic surrogate search, per-candidate output audits, violation search over input/load-history spans, and verified reporting.

Authors: Diab W. Abueidda, Bilal Ahmed, Panos Pantidis, Mostafa E. Mobasher

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Mechanistic Interpretability for Neural Networks: Circuits, Sparse Features and Symbolic Reasoning
item.description:
Priority: 24/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 16, Paper 35, Empirical 10, Reproducibility 5, Manuscript 55

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI, journal or conference venue, citation count, open-source code

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-07-08, 20 pages.; Abstract frames the article as a comprehensive overview, not as a new empirical study.; Covers Transformer circuit analysis, SAEs, transcoders, steering vectors, causal interventions, and neurosymbolic AI.; No DOI, journal reference, code, data, baselines, or evaluation metrics are present in the supplied metadata.

TL;DR: This 20-page arXiv v1 overview surveys mechanistic interpretability for neural networks, covering Transformer circuits, sparse feature decomposition, steering/causal interventions, and links to neurosymbolic rule extraction.

Problem: The paper studies how to make modern neural networks less opaque by reverse-engineering their internal algorithms rather than relying only on input-output explanations.

Method: It appears to be a survey/framework-style overview of mechanistic interpretability topics: Transformer circuit analysis, residual streams, attention mechanisms, induction heads, superposition/polysemanticity, Sparse Autoencoders, transcoders, steering vectors, causal interventions, and neurosymbolic translation of neural representations into logical rules.

Key contribution: The abstract’s novelty is integrative rather than empirical: it connects circuit-level interpretability, sparse feature methods, behavior control via causal interventions, and neurosymbolic reasoning in one mechanistic interpretability overview.

Main result: No direct empirical result is reported in the abstract; it describes topics covered rather than experiments, benchmarks, or measured outcomes.

Limitation: The supplied metadata/abstract provides no evidence of new experiments, baselines, ablations, robustness checks, code, public data, affiliations, DOI, venue, or citation signals, so the strength of the contribution cannot be assessed beyond the stated survey scope.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query "causality" because the abstract explicitly discusses causal interventions and mechanistic methods for identifying and modifying internal model components that drive behavior.

Tags: mechanistic-interpretability, causal-interventions, transformer-circuits, sparse-autoencoders, neurosymbolic-ai, survey

Figure note: A pipeline or framework figure would be useful if available, showing how circuit analysis, sparse feature decomposition, causal interventions/steering, and symbolic rule extraction connect.

Authors: Pranav Sawant, Jakub Krejčí

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Distributed Sparse Interventions in Language Models
item.description:
Priority: 49/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 20, Paper 35, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 20, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index signals, DOI, conference, or journal venue, open-source code, public data or benchmark details, named baselines

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08.; Abstract reports neuron-specific nonlinear effects not captured by current steering approaches.; Introduces Distributed Sparse Interventions for sparse neuron sets across layers.; Claims task behavior can be activated by intervening on as few as 0.01% of neurons.

TL;DR: The paper proposes Distributed Sparse Interventions, a neuron-level causal intervention method for language models that identifies tiny distributed neuron sets whose nonlinear interactions can activate task-relevant behavior.

Problem: The paper studies how specific model components causally influence task behavior in language models, especially where global linear steering directions fail to capture neuron-specific nonlinear effects.

Method: It introduces Distributed Sparse Interventions (DSI), which searches for sparse neuron sets across layers while accounting for nonlinearities and neuron interactions, then intervenes on those neurons to elicit task-relevant computations.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is moving from global activation-space steering directions to sparse, distributed neuron-basis interventions that model nonlinear and cross-layer interactions, enabling set-level analysis of task-related neurons.

Main result: From the abstract, DSI activates task behavior in instruction-tuned language models across a range of tasks while intervening on as few as 0.01% of neurons.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify model families, datasets, baselines, metrics, ablations, robustness checks, code availability, or whether the effects transfer across architectures.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it explicitly uses interventions to study causal influence of neurons on language-model task behavior.

Tags: language-models, causal-interventions, mechanistic-interpretability, model-steering, sparse-interventions, neurons

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's framework or pipeline figure showing how DSI identifies sparse neuron sets across layers and applies interventions to activate task behavior.

Authors: Maximilian S. Ernst, Lorenz Linhardt, Aaron Peikert, Oliver Eberle

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Embracing Spillover: Spatial Effects in Experiments
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 14, Paper 50, Empirical 30, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 80

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index evidence, DOI or venue signal, conference or journal acceptance, citation count, open-source code

Score evidence: Abstract states that spillover effects violate independent-unit assumptions in spatial experiments.; Defines causal estimands as linear functionals of a spillover kernel.; Claims kernel shape is identifiable but level is not under cluster randomization with fixed exposure set size.; Provides exact bias decomposition and a scalar level-leverage diagnostic.

TL;DR: The paper develops a causal-inference framework for spatial experiments with spillover, showing that common estimands depend on a spillover kernel whose shape can be identified under certain cluster-randomized designs but whose level requires an anchoring assumption or design augmentation.

Problem: The paper studies causal estimation in spatial experiments where interventions affect nearby or otherwise spatially related units, creating interference that invalidates standard no-spillover trial analyses.

Method: It formulates direct, indirect, total, and dose-response effects as linear functionals of a spillover kernel under cluster randomization with fixed exposure set size, then analyzes identification, bias, anchoring assumptions, and design augmentations within that framework.

Key contribution: The main novelty is the explicit identification result that the kernel shape is identifiable but its level is aliased with the intercept and direct effect, plus an exact pre-data bias decomposition and a scalar 'level leverage' diagnostic for estimand sensitivity to the unidentified level.

Main result: From the abstract, the main result is theoretical: any anchored estimator's bias decomposes exactly into level bias, kernel-shape misspecification error, and geometry-driven leakage, and conventional cluster-trial analysis emerges as a special implicit-anchor case with closed-form contamination bias.

Limitation: The abstract does not report empirical validation, simulations, real-data examples, code, or robustness checks; evidence appears primarily theoretical from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it addresses causal estimands, interference, spillover, identification, and bias in spatial experiments, a focused causal-inference setting.

Tags: causal inference, spatial experiments, interference, spillover, cluster randomization, identification, bias decomposition, experimental design

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram showing the spillover kernel, anchored level, bias decomposition terms, and how design augmentations recover the unidentified level.

Authors: Samuel I. Watson, Penny Hancock, Jack Fraser-Govil, Kieran DeWalt, Thomas A. Smith

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Prompt-Adapter Context Routing for Parameter-Efficient Multi-Shot Long Video Extrapolation
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 8, Paper 42, Empirical 82, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 74

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI, journal or conference venue, open-source code, public dataset or benchmark names

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-07 in cs.CV and cs.AI.; Abstract claims a frozen text-to-video diffusion transformer augmented with low-rank temporal adapters.; Uses recursive prompt bank storing entity, location, action, and style prompts from previous shots.; Reports comparison against several baseline families across six multi-shot and long-video benchmarks.

TL;DR: PACR-Video proposes lightweight prompt-routed temporal adapters for frozen text-to-video diffusion transformers to improve coherent multi-shot long video extrapolation.

Problem: The paper studies long-horizon multi-shot video extrapolation, where generated future shots must preserve recurring entities, scene structure, visual style, and causal/narrative progression without expensive full generator fine-tuning.

Method: PACR-Video freezes a text-to-video diffusion transformer and adds low-rank temporal adapters conditioned on learned shot-role prompt tokens. It builds a recursive prompt bank of entity, location, action, and style prompts from previous shots, routes them through adapter gates based on predicted narrative dependencies, and trains with a Shot-Local/Story-Global objective combining next-shot reconstruction, cross-shot identity contrast, and prompt sparsity regularization.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed in the abstract is combining compact recursive prompt memory, dependency-aware prompt routing, and low-rank temporal adapters to provide controllable long-video capacity without full model fine-tuning.

Main result: The abstract reports that PACR-Video outperforms text-to-video, tuning-based, memory-augmented, streaming, and recursive-context baselines across six multi-shot/long-video benchmarks on quality, semantic alignment, identity consistency, temporal smoothness, motion stability, transition coherence, and human preference; exact numbers are not provided.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative effect sizes, benchmark names, architecture details, code/data availability, ablations, robustness tests, or evidence that the causal progression claim reflects formal causal modeling rather than narrative coherence.

Relevance: Relevant to the query 'causality' mainly through causal progression and predicted narrative dependencies in video extrapolation, but it appears closer to long-video generation/coherence than to causal inference or causal discovery.

Tags: text-to-video, long video generation, diffusion transformers, parameter-efficient tuning, prompt routing, temporal adapters, narrative coherence, causal progression

Figure note: The comment indicates 2 figures, but no direct image URL is supplied. A useful image would be the PACR-Video pipeline showing the frozen diffusion transformer, recursive prompt bank, adapter gates, and shot-local/story-global training flow.

Authors: Anna Córdoba, Adam Puente Tercero, Nerea Angulo Hijo, Mar Linares Tercero, Julia Barrientos, Ainhoa Miranda, Jesús Olivera

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Direct and efficient estimation of bilinear forms in staggered tensor panels
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 30, Paper 45, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index signals, DOI or venue, citation count, open-source code, public data availability

Score evidence: Abstract explicitly links the structured missingness pattern to staggered adoption designs common in causal inference.; Proposes a spectral algorithm that pools across tensor layers and estimates the functional directly.; Claims non-asymptotic MSE bounds and local minimax lower bounds matching up to constants.; Reports experiments on simulated and real-world datasets, but without abstract-level details.

TL;DR: The paper studies direct estimation of bilinear forms from noisy, partially observed Tucker2 tensor panels with staggered-adoption missingness, proposing a pooled spectral estimator with non-asymptotic and minimax guarantees.

Problem: Estimating a target bilinear functional from noisy tensor data when observations are incomplete in structured patterns motivated by staggered adoption designs, without needing full tensor completion.

Method: The authors analyze a four-block missingness pattern, propose a spectral algorithm that pools information across tensor layers and targets the bilinear form directly, then extend it to general staggered adoption designs through an anchored four-block reduction.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a direct functional-estimation approach for staggered tensor panels, with theory showing a phase transition in the number of layers where pooling becomes beneficial, plus matching local minimax lower bounds up to constants.

Main result: The abstract reports non-asymptotic mean squared error bounds, local minimax lower bounds matching up to constants, analogous guarantees for general staggered adoption, and validation on simulated and real-world datasets; no numerical results are provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify empirical baselines, datasets, metrics, code availability, robustness checks, or practical assumptions required by the Tucker2 and missingness models.

Relevance: Relevant to the causal inference feed because the missingness structure is explicitly motivated by staggered adoption designs, a common setup in causal inference, although the paper is primarily statistical theory for tensor panel estimation rather than an applied causal identification paper.

Tags: causal inference, staggered adoption, tensor panels, Tucker2 model, bilinear forms, spectral algorithm, non-asymptotic theory, minimax lower bound

Figure note: A useful image would be the framework or pipeline figure showing the four-block missingness pattern, anchored reduction for staggered adoption, and how the spectral estimator pools layers to estimate the bilinear form directly.

TL;DR: The paper applies unsupervised dictionary learning as a post hoc interpretability layer for end-to-end autonomous driving models, using concept-level causal interventions to expose and improve trajectory decisions.

Problem: End-to-end autonomous driving models are increasingly complex and opaque, which makes it difficult to detect undesired or erroneous learned driving behavior.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element claimed in the abstract is connecting learned interpretability concepts not only to model outputs but also to causal interventions that can manipulate and correct driving decisions.

Main result: The abstract reports measurable improvements in overall driving performance after targeted concept-level interventions, but does not provide datasets, metrics, baselines, or effect sizes.

item.title: MobileWan: Closing the Quality Gap for Mobile Video Diffusion
item.description:
Priority: 62/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 56, Paper 55, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: Explicit author affiliations, Citation count or h-index metadata, DOI, journal, or conference venue, Open-source code or license statement, Exact device and hardware configuration, Baseline comparison details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-07 in cs.CV.; Abstract starts from Wan2.2-5B and claims deployment of a 5B-scale video diffusion model on a commercial mobile device.; Reported result: 5-second 480x832 videos at 16 FPS in 20 seconds latency and VBench score 83.79.; Project page is listed, but the abstract does not state code, data, or detailed benchmark setup.

TL;DR: MobileWan reformulates and compresses a 5B video diffusion transformer so it can run high-quality 5-second video generation on commercial mobile hardware with constant-memory, causal/autoregressive inference.

Problem: The paper studies how to close the quality gap between server-scale video diffusion models and mobile video generation systems, where prior mobile models use much smaller parameter budgets and therefore have limited fidelity and motion coherence.

Method: Starting from Wan2.2-5B, the authors use recurrence distillation to convert generation into chunk-wise autoregressive inference with constant-memory attention, combine it with causal linear attention, add learnable binary-gated attention-head pruning with sparsity and distillation losses, and use sampling-step distillation plus memory-optimized VAE decoding.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that a server-scale 5B video diffusion transformer can be deployed on memory-constrained mobile hardware by making inference RNN-like and combining structured compression, pruning, step distillation, and optimized decoding, instead of designing a much smaller mobile-only model.

Main result: The abstract reports 5-second 480x832 videos at 16 FPS generated in 20 seconds end-to-end latency on a commercial mobile device, with a VBench score of 83.79 and a claimed new state of the art in mobile video generation.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify the exact mobile device, baselines, ablations, robustness tests, human evaluation, code availability, or whether the reported latency and VBench results generalize across prompts and hardware.

Relevance: The match to the configured query "causality" appears to come from causal linear attention and autoregressive chunk-wise generation, not from causal inference or causal modeling; it is relevant if the feed tracks causal/causal-attention mechanisms in generative architectures, but likely peripheral for causal reasoning research.

Tags: video diffusion, mobile inference, causal linear attention, recurrent distillation, model compression, attention head pruning, edge AI

Figure note: A useful image would be the MobileWan system pipeline showing recurrence distillation, chunk-wise causal/RNN-style inference, attention-head pruning, sampling-step distillation, and mobile VAE decoding.

TL;DR: SocaSim proposes an LLM-based multi-agent simulation framework for operationalizing Putnam's Social Capital Theory through evolving networks, trust dynamics, norm propagation, repeated collective-action experiments, and counterfactual interventions.

Problem: The paper studies how to model and test Putnam's Social Capital Theory when traditional empirical methods have limited control and replicability, and when existing LLM social simulations are behavior-driven rather than explicitly aligned to social-science theory.

Method: It introduces SocaSim, a theory-aligned LLM multi-agent simulation environment with social network evolution, trust dynamics, norm propagation, repeated collective-action experiments, round-by-round process tracing, and counterfactual interventions; it also applies the framework to smart elderly care adaptation challenges.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is a theory-to-simulation pipeline that makes Putnam-style macro social-capital patterns observable while tracing micro-level causal pathways among networks, trust, and norms inside an LLM-agent environment.

Main result: From the abstract, the simulations reportedly reproduce Putnam's macro-level patterns and show strong group-level human-agent alignment, but no exact metrics, datasets, baselines, or statistical tests are provided in the supplied metadata.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code availability, implementation details, quantitative alignment metrics, baseline comparisons, ablations, robustness checks, external-validation evidence, affiliations, citation signals, DOI, or peer-reviewed venue; causal claims appear simulation-based rather than evidence of real-world causal identification.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it explicitly claims to trace micro-level causal pathways and use counterfactual interventions, though its relevance is stronger for causal process simulation than for standard causal inference or identification methods.

Tags: LLM agents, multi-agent simulation, social capital, causality, counterfactual interventions, social simulation, cs.CL, cs.AI

item.description:

TL;DR: NEST proposes a graph-theoretic ontology for representing cognitive states, beliefs, working memory, conflicts, and belief updates, with causal relations included as one of several typed edge classes.

Problem: The paper studies how to represent cognition as structured state formation and transformation across concepts, episodes, percepts, task contexts, working memory, and durable beliefs.

Method: It proposes NEST, a formal graph-based representational framework using typed, weighted graphs with nested subgraph payloads, six edge-relation classes, separate belief and working-memory graphs, grounding mechanisms, conflict catalogs, belief-update operators, and diagnostic graph functionals.

Key contribution: The novelty claimed in the abstract is a unified representational substrate that can express belief, working memory, self-related processing, task instantiation, failure modes, and mappings to existing cognitive architectures such as ACT-R, Soar, Sigma, Global Workspace Theory, semantic networks, Theory-Theory, and chunking without adding new primitives.

Main result: No empirical result is reported in the abstract; the contribution is explicitly described as foundational rather than a finished empirical model.

Limitation: The abstract provides no experiments, baselines, validation tasks, implementation, code, data, quantitative metrics, affiliations, venue signal, DOI, or citation evidence. It also frames the work as a substrate for later empirical and computational work rather than as a tested model.

Relevance: Relevant to the query "causality" because causal edges are one of NEST's six relation classes and the framework includes belief grounding, conflict handling, and belief-update operators, but the paper appears broader than causal inference or causal discovery specifically.

Tags: graph ontology, cognitive architecture, belief update, working memory, causal relations, foundational framework

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram showing nested belief and working-memory graphs, typed edge classes, grounding/conflict flow, and belief-update operators.

item.title: Learning Sparsest Linear Causal DAGs with Latent Confounders via Higher-Order Cumulants
item.description:
Priority: 46/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 40, Paper 45, Empirical 58, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 65

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation count or h-index, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, open-source code, public data details, baseline names

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-07-07 in cs.LG.; Abstract states the method recovers the sparsest DAG in LvLiNGAM using higher-order cumulants.; Abstract claims no restriction on the number of latent confounders.; Abstract reports simulation studies and real-data analyses with superior finite-sample performance.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a finite-sample method using higher-order cumulants to recover the unique sparsest DAG in linear non-Gaussian acyclic causal models with latent confounders.

Problem: Recovering the exact causal DAG in LvLiNGAM with latent confounders is difficult because the model is identifiable only up to an observational equivalence class, and finite-sample recovery of the unique sparsest DAG remains challenging.

Method: The abstract describes a finite-sample recovery method based on higher-order cumulants for learning the sparsest linear causal DAG, without restricting the number of latent confounders.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is an explicit finite-sample procedure for recovering the unique sparsest DAG in LvLiNGAM while allowing an arbitrary number of latent confounders, unlike prior asymptotically consistent methods with stronger restrictions.

Main result: The abstract reports that simulations and real-data analyses show superior finite-sample performance compared with existing approaches, but it does not provide datasets, metrics, effect sizes, or statistical details.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify algorithmic assumptions beyond LvLiNGAM, computational complexity, exact baselines, robustness checks, ablations, code availability, or quantitative empirical results.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the causality feed because it targets causal DAG learning under latent confounding, a central problem in causal discovery.

Tags: causality, causal discovery, LiNGAM, latent confounders, DAG learning, higher-order cumulants, finite-sample methods

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or pipeline figure showing how higher-order cumulants are estimated from finite samples and used to select the sparsest DAG within the observational equivalence class.

item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1, 49 pages, categories stat.ME and econ.EM.; Abstract targets recommendation systems and chatbots using stochastic algorithms.; Method requires logging an unexposed item and a probability ratio for each user.; Abstract claims identification even with unobserved confounders affecting preferences and item-pair identity.

TL;DR: The paper proposes an observational-data method for identifying causal effects of content features in recommendation systems and chatbots driven by stochastic exposure algorithms.

Problem: It studies how to estimate whether content characteristics causally affect outcomes such as engagement when exposed content is tailored to users and varies along many dimensions, making standard observational comparisons confounded.

Method: The proposed method uses logged stochastic exposure data plus, for each user, an unexposed candidate item and an estimated probability ratio comparing how likely the unexposed item was to be shown versus the exposed item.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is an identification and estimator strategy that can recover feature-level causal effects even with unobserved confounders affecting user preferences and the considered exposed/unexposed item pair, using data elements not required by standard approaches.

Main result: The abstract states an identification result for causal effects under the proposed logging setup, but does not report empirical experiments, numerical estimates, datasets, or benchmark comparisons.

Limitation: Evidence in the supplied abstract is mainly methodological; empirical validation, practical logging burden, sensitivity to probability-ratio estimation error, baselines, ablations, and robustness checks are not described.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query "causality" because it directly addresses causal effect identification and estimation under stochastic algorithmic assignment in recommender and chatbot settings.

Tags: causal inference, stochastic algorithms, recommendation systems, chatbots, observational data, content features, identification, stat.ME

item.title: Controlling Tool Use with Heading-Specific Activation Steering
item.description:
Priority: 48/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 45, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: No affiliation metadata supplied., No citation or h-index metadata supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference signal supplied., No code or repository signal supplied., No public data details supplied., No baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, or metric details in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-07-07.; Evaluated across five open-source models and three domains.; Reports bidirectional causal control over tool-invocation behavior.; Finds diffuse, bimodal alignment rather than clean linear structure.

TL;DR: The paper studies whether LLM tool-invocation behavior can be causally controlled via activation steering and finds that heading-specific steering vectors can suppress or induce tool use despite lacking clean linear internal geometry.

Problem: Tool-augmented LLMs often call external tools unnecessarily; the paper asks whether the decision to invoke a tool has a stable internal representation that can be extracted and manipulated at inference time.

Method: The authors extract steering vectors from heading-anchor positions and test whether these vectors causally control tool-invocation behavior across five open-source models and three domains, followed by geometric analysis of alignment patterns and cross-tool feature overlap.

Key contribution: It reports bidirectional causal control over tool use while arguing that the underlying representation is not a simple linear encoding: tool-invocation activations show diffuse, bimodal alignment and tool-type-specific internal signatures.

Main result: From the abstract: steering vectors suppress unnecessary tool use most effectively in domains where parametric reasoning is sufficient, across five open-source models and three domains.

Limitation: The abstract leaves open why geometrically irregular representations still yield causal control, and does not specify datasets, metrics, baselines, ablations, code availability, or robustness tests.

Relevance: The paper matches the configured query 'causality' because it explicitly studies bidirectional causal control of LLM tool-use behavior through activation interventions.

Tags: activation steering, tool-augmented LLMs, causal control, mechanistic interpretability, tool use, representation geometry

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or framework figure showing heading-anchor extraction, steering-vector construction, intervention during generation, and the geometric alignment analysis across tool types.

item.title: Faithfulness to Refusal: A Causal Audit of Neuron Selectors
item.description:
Priority: 55/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 18, Paper 42, Empirical 75, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 68

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index signals, DOI, journal, or conference venue, citation count or influential-citation signal, open-source code, public data or benchmark names

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-06 with no DOI, journal reference, or conference signal in supplied metadata.; Abstract reports audits across five LLMs.; Abstract names activation and magnitude-based baselines plus layer-matched random controls.; Abstract reports behavior outcomes: refusal on hate and crime, benign over-refusal, and language-model fluency.

TL;DR: The paper proposes direct causal audits for neuron-selector faithfulness in LLM refusal behavior, showing that attribution-based selectors can identify causally effective neuron rows while stability-based proxies can be misleading.

Problem: Attribution scores are widely used to choose neuron rows for pruning, interpretability, and safety editing, but the abstract argues that these selectors are rarely tested for whether the chosen rows are actually causally important.

Method: The paper uses two paired audits based on one-shot neuron-row zeroing: a language-modeling audit for identifying dispensable rows, and a behavior-level audit driven by a contrastive harmful-versus-benign signal to test whether selected rows can induce refusal behavior.

Key contribution: It reframes neuron-selector evaluation as a direct causal intervention test and reports that highly rank-stable selectors can be causally weak, while refusal behavior can be installed through multiple largely disjoint sufficient row sets rather than a single unique mechanism.

Main result: From the abstract: attribution methods outperform activation and magnitude baselines at finding dispensable rows across five LLMs; contrastively attributed rows are sufficient to install refusal on hate and crime prompts with low benign over-refusal and preserved fluency, while layer-matched random controls fail.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention code release, dataset details, statistical uncertainty, affiliations, external validation, or whether the audits cover behaviors beyond hate and crime refusal.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the query 'causality' because it tests whether attribution-selected neuron rows are causally responsible for language-model behavior rather than merely correlated or rank-stable.

Tags: causality, LLM interpretability, neuron attribution, refusal behavior, model editing, safety, causal intervention

Figure note: A useful figure would show the paired audit pipeline: selector scoring, neuron-row zeroing intervention, language-modeling audit, contrastive harmful-versus-benign refusal audit, and comparison against random or magnitude/activation baselines.

item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-06.; Abstract reports experiments on three open-weight 7-8B models and seven completion-style datasets.; Uses minimal-capacity linear probes on frozen hidden states.; Reports transfer from natural-language datasets to controlled synthetic completions and comparison to a statistical baseline.

TL;DR: The paper argues that 7-8B open-weight LLMs linearly encode an approximate estimate of remaining output length in their hidden states, including before generation begins.

Problem: It studies whether autoregressive LLMs maintain an internal estimate of how much response remains despite generating text one token at a time.

Method: The authors train minimal-capacity linear probes on frozen hidden states from three open-weight 7-8B models across seven completion-style datasets, then test prompt-state decodability, cross-dataset transfer, synthetic-completion transfer, and behavior around curated retraction/restart events.

Key contribution: The novelty is evidence for a broadly transferable, linearly decodable remaining-length signal, separated from exact counting claims and framed as a plan-like representation rather than a proven causal control mechanism.

Main result: The abstract reports that total response length is linearly decodable from the prompt's final hidden state before output, natural-language-trained probe directions transfer broadly and outperform a statistical baseline, and probe estimates rise during qualitative retraction/restart examples.

Limitation: The abstract does not report aggregate results for the retraction analysis, causal interventions, full metric values, ablations, code, public data, affiliations, citation signals, or peer-reviewed venue information.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causality' mainly because it explicitly distinguishes decodable internal representations from causal use; however, it appears more about mechanistic probing and planning representations than causal intervention or causal inference.

item.title: Causal-RetiGraph: Cross-Cohort Retinal Support and Same-Subject Pathway Analysis for Diabetic Retinopathy
item.description:

TL;DR: Causal-RetiGraph links retinal image-derived graph phenotypes with NHANES pathway modeling to connect diabetic retinopathy lesion evidence, vascular biomarkers, and systemic disease pathways.

Problem: The paper studies how to move beyond accurate diabetic retinopathy image grading toward interpretable connections among local retinal lesions, retinal vascular structure, and systemic microvascular disease pathways.

Method: It proposes Causal-RetiGraph, a two-fold framework: a retinal-image fold builds an interpretable X1234 phenotype from vessel maps, lesion evidence, image embeddings, and AutoMorph biomarkers through spatial X12 and Jacobian X34 branches; an NHANES fold models systemic exposures, covariates, same-subject retinal mediator variables R*, and downstream outcome families.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed in the abstract is the separation of image-derived retinal support via X1234 from participant-level same-subject pathway summaries via R*, enabling retinal graph phenotypes to prioritize systemic DR pathways without conflating image evidence with mediation.

Main result: The retinal fold reports 0.9055 binary DR accuracy, 0.9711 AUROC, and 0.8312 quadratic weighted kappa for graded DR. NHANES analysis identifies HbA1c, urine albumin, pulse pressure, fasting glucose, and systolic blood pressure as the strongest binary DR anchors, with glycaemic-renal and glycaemic-haemodynamic pathways as the clearest mediator-style signals.

Limitation: The abstract does not report dataset sizes, external validation details, exact baselines, ablations, statistical uncertainty, robustness checks, code availability, or affiliations. Causal strength is also unclear from the abstract because the wording emphasizes pathway prioritization and mediator-style signals rather than confirmed causal effects.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query "causality" because it frames diabetic retinopathy analysis around causal/pathway modeling, mediator-style signals, and cross-cohort links between image phenotypes and systemic exposures.

TL;DR: UNIVERSE proposes a single mask-modulated Diffusion Transformer that jointly learns future video latents and ego-trajectory tokens so video-based world dynamics supervision transfers more directly to autonomous-driving trajectory generation.

Problem: The paper studies how to transfer the benefits of future video prediction and temporal-causal scene modeling into trajectory planning for autonomous driving, especially under cross-domain generalization.

Method: It introduces UNIVERSE, a unified video-action model with shared generative parameters for future video latents and ego-trajectory tokens, plus a Modality-Decoupling Visibility Mask that shares historical context while blocking attention between future video targets and trajectory targets.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is replacing cascaded or dual-DiT video/action designs with a single DiT whose video co-training directly shapes trajectory denoising, while the visibility mask prevents future-target leakage and enables trajectory-only inference.

Main result: From the abstract, UNIVERSE reaches 91.0 PDMS on NAVSIM versus 89.6 for a Two-DiT variant, reports zero-shot transfer to nuScenes and Bench2Drive without fine-tuning, and achieves a 4.3x speedup for trajectory-only inference compared with joint video-action rollout.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide code, data-release status, full baseline comparisons, statistical uncertainty, detailed zero-shot transfer numbers, or robustness evidence; DOI, venue, affiliations, and citation signals are also absent from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the query 'causality' because it frames future video prediction as supervision for scene dynamics and temporal causality, and proposes a causal visibility mask to avoid leakage between future video and trajectory targets.

Tags: autonomous-driving, world-action-models, diffusion-transformer, video-prediction, trajectory-planning, causality, zero-shot-transfer

item.title: Identification and Bounding of Central Moments of Causal Effects Using Marginal Moments Information
item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-07-06 in stat.ME and stat.ML.; Abstract focuses on central moments of individual causal effects and treatment-effect heterogeneity.; Claims use of marginal central moments of potential outcomes instead of full marginal distributions.; Abstract mentions two empirical case studies but gives no numerical results.

TL;DR: The paper studies how to identify or bound central moments of individual causal effects using only marginal moment information about potential outcomes, aiming to characterize treatment-effect heterogeneity with weaker inputs than full marginal distributions.

Problem: Average causal effects hide the shape and heterogeneity of individual causal effects; the paper asks when central moments of the ICE distribution can be identified or bounded from limited potential-outcome information.

Method: It proposes identification and bounding results for central moments of individual causal effects based only on marginal central moments of each potential outcome, rather than full marginal potential-outcome distributions.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed in the abstract is replacing the stronger requirement of knowing full marginal distributions of potential outcomes with the weaker and often more accessible requirement of knowing marginal moment information.

Main result: The abstract reports theoretical identification/bounding results and says they are illustrated with two empirical case studies, but it does not provide quantitative empirical findings or benchmark comparisons.

Limitation: The supplied abstract does not state assumptions, tightness conditions, datasets, code availability, baselines, ablations, robustness checks, or quantitative case-study outcomes.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it addresses identification and bounding of causal-effect heterogeneity, a core causal inference problem beyond average treatment effects.

Tags: causal inference, individual causal effects, treatment effect heterogeneity, potential outcomes, moment bounds, identification

item.title: Do Vision-Language-Action Models Mean What They Say? On the Role of Faithfulness in Embodied Reasoning
item.description:
Priority: 60/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 28, Paper 50, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 76

Missing quality signals: No DOI, journal, or conference venue in metadata., No affiliations, citation counts, h-index, or prior author-topic metadata supplied., No code repository or license stated, only a project page URL., No dataset names, benchmark details, metric definitions, ablations, or statistical robustness in the abstract., No explicit data availability or implementation details in the supplied metadata., No direct limitation discussion visible in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.RO and cs.AI, published 2026-07-06.; Abstract distinguishes functional reasoning from faithful reasoning in embodied Chain-of-Thought.; Reports human evaluation of a SoTA autonomous-driving reasoning model.; Proposes Pinocchio, a learned critic scoring observation grounding and stepwise coherence.

TL;DR: The paper studies whether embodied Chain-of-Thought in VLA driving models is causally faithful to policy decisions and proposes a learned faithfulness critic, Pinocchio, to improve grounded and coherent reasoning through RL post-training.

Problem: Embodied Chain-of-Thought can improve interpretability and performance in robot/VLA policies, but the abstract argues that verbal reasoning may be functionally useful without faithfully reflecting the policy's internal decision process, especially when intermediate reasoning is ungrounded, inconsistent, or confounded.

Method: The authors distinguish functional from faithful reasoning, evaluate a state-of-the-art autonomous-driving reasoning model with human judgments, train a critic called Pinocchio to score observation grounding and stepwise coherence, and use that critic as a dense reward for reinforcement-learning post-training of an embodied planner.

Key contribution: A behavioral surrogate for embodied reasoning faithfulness that targets causal alignment between observations, reasoning steps, and actions, rather than only optimizing trajectory error or standard alignment objectives.

Main result: Across withheld driving benchmarks, the post-trained planner improves faithfulness by 4% over a SoTA alignment baseline and 18% over a trajectory-error post-training baseline while maintaining competitive downstream performance; on synthetic OOD counterfactual tests, it improves responsiveness to rare scenarios by 1.6x relative to a SoTA policy.

Limitation: The abstract does not report dataset names, exact metrics, model architecture details, ablations, statistical uncertainty, code availability, or whether the learned critic itself is causally validated beyond behavioral scoring.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query "causality" because it explicitly studies whether VLA reasoning traces preserve causal links to action prediction and proposes a reward-based method to improve faithfulness under confounding and counterfactual scenarios.

Tags: causality, vision-language-action models, embodied reasoning, chain-of-thought, robotics, autonomous driving, reinforcement learning, faithfulness

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's framework diagram showing how Pinocchio scores observation grounding and stepwise coherence, then supplies dense rewards for RL post-training of the embodied planner.

item.title: Retroactive Chain-of-Thought (RetroCoT): Forensic Reconstruction Prompts as a Safety Diagnostic Across Model Generations
item.description:
Priority: 48/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 35, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 68

Missing quality signals: author affiliation, citation count or h-index, DOI, journal or conference venue, code repository, full prompts or implementation details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-07-06 with no DOI or journal reference in supplied metadata.; Single listed author: Samira Hajizadeh; no affiliation or citation metadata supplied.; Abstract reports AdvBench n=50 and direct-request baselines for GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini.; Abstract reports GPT-5-family single-turn refusal and multi-turn continuation/control ASR results.

TL;DR: The paper studies how LLM safety refusals vary under semantically equivalent but pragmatically reframed harmful requests, introducing RetroCoT as a forensic-reconstruction prompting diagnostic.

Problem: Current safety alignment evaluations usually test direct harmful requests, but the paper argues that models may comply when the same harmful intent is framed in a different pragmatic register.

Method: The paper proposes Retroactive Chain-of-Thought, a single-turn prompt framing harmful objectives as forensic reverse reconstruction of an outcome that has already occurred, and evaluates it on AdvBench across GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5-family models, plus adversarial feedback/control turns.

Key contribution: Its main novelty is treating pragmatic register invariance as a safety diagnostic and showing that forensic reconstruction framing can bypass models that refuse direct requests, while newer models appear to cover that specific register but remain vulnerable to related continuation framing.

Main result: From the abstract: RetroCoT reaches 58% ASR on GPT-4o and 52% on GPT-4o-mini versus 0% and 4% direct-request baselines; GPT-5-family models refuse single-turn RetroCoT, but an adversarial feedback/continuation turn raises ASR to 48% on GPT-5.4-mini and 94% on GPT-4o, with a control reaching 85% on GPT-5.4-mini.

Limitation: Evidence is limited to the abstract: no code, prompt set, full model list, statistical uncertainty, robustness analysis, affiliation, DOI, venue, or external replication is supplied. The abstract is truncated, so some methodology and limitation details are unavailable.

Relevance: Matched the query "causality" because the proposed framing asks models to reconstruct causal chains behind harmful outcomes; it is more directly relevant to LLM safety, adversarial prompting, and causal/pragmatic reasoning than to core causal inference.

Tags: LLM safety, adversarial prompting, jailbreak diagnostics, pragmatic framing, causal reconstruction, AdvBench

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram contrasting direct harmful request evaluation with RetroCoT forensic reverse-causal-chain framing, plus the adversarial continuation/control flow across model generations.

item.title: A General Exposure-Mapping-Agnostic Framework for Causal Inference under Interference
item.description:
Priority: 66/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 50, Paper 68, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: Affiliations not supplied., Citation count and h-index signals not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference reference supplied., No code repository or implementation artifact mentioned., No public data source mentioned., No exact simulation metrics, sample sizes, or numerical results in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-06 in stat.ME.; Abstract states the framework avoids exposure mapping assumptions, exclusion of cross-cluster interference, and Bernoulli treatment assignments.; Abstract claims a complete characterization of linear weighted estimators for identifying network causal effects under interference.; Abstract reports central limit theorems and conservative variance estimators for most estimators.

TL;DR: The paper develops an exposure-mapping-agnostic design-based framework for causal inference under network interference in two-stage cluster-randomized experiments, with a characterization of linear weighted estimators and supporting asymptotic and simulation evidence.

Problem: It studies how to identify and estimate network causal effects under interference in clustered experiments when units are connected across a network and standard simplifying assumptions such as fixed exposure mappings, no cross-cluster interference, or Bernoulli treatment assignment may fail.

Method: The paper proposes a general design-based framework for two-stage randomization on networks and characterizes linear weighted estimators, in the Godambe sense, that identify causal effects under interference. It also derives central limit theorems and conservative variance estimators for most estimators in the class.

Key contribution: The main novelty is an exposure-mapping-agnostic framework that covers cluster experiments with network interference and non-Bernoulli assignments, plus new linear weighted estimators including cluster-agnostic estimators whose convergence rates are independent of the number of clusters and can attain root-N rates.

Main result: According to the abstract, the new estimators have improved theoretical guarantees and superior finite-sample performance over standard inverse-probability-of-treatment weighting, supported by extensive simulations across interference structures. Exact numerical gains are not provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code, public data, exact simulation settings, quantitative effect sizes, real-data validation, ablations, robustness checks, DOI, venue, or affiliations. Limitations are not explicitly discussed.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it directly addresses causal effect identification and estimation under interference, a central problem in modern causal inference for networked and clustered experiments.

Tags: causal inference, interference, cluster randomized experiments, network causal effects, design-based inference, linear weighted estimators, two-stage randomization, asymptotic theory

Figure note: A useful figure would show the two-stage cluster-randomized network setting, how interference crosses clusters, and where the proposed linear weighted estimator subclasses fit relative to IPTW; no direct image URL is available from the supplied metadata.

Method: The abstract describes an empirical scholarly-migration analysis of NLP research from 2010 to 2026, comparing established and newer authors across pre- and post-LLM periods, with causal inference techniques used to estimate citation effects of venue choice.

item.description:

TL;DR: The paper develops design-based causal inference theory for experiments where randomly assigned individuals interact in treatment-assigned groups, including fixed or randomly formed groups and settings with or without interference.

Problem: Standard experimental inference is less straightforward when individuals are assigned to interacting groups and outcomes may depend on other group members' assignments, so the paper studies what causal estimands and variance estimators are valid under different group-formation and interference regimes.

Method: It gives a design-based inference framework for group interaction experiments, characterizing the estimand targeted in each scenario and deriving asymptotic distributions for commonly used estimators under a sparse-sampling regime.

Key contribution: The abstract claims a novel coupling strategy for proving asymptotic results in complex design-based experiments, plus a unified treatment of fixed versus randomly formed groups and interference versus no-interference cases.

Main result: From the abstract: cluster-robust inference remains consistent under interference and supports valid inference on marginalized exposure effects; when interference is absent and groups are randomly formed, individual-level heteroskedasticity-robust inference is sufficient for the average treatment effect.

Limitation: No empirical application, simulation evidence, finite-sample performance, code, data, affiliations, DOI, venue, or robustness evidence is provided in the supplied metadata/abstract.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it addresses causal estimands, interference, randomized experimental design, and valid inference for treatment effects in group-interaction settings.
