item.title: Conditional Co-Ablation: Recovering Self-Repair Backups in Transformer Circuits
item.description:
Priority: 58/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 34, Paper 52, Empirical 82, Reproducibility 38, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: No affiliation or institution metadata supplied., No citation count or h-index metadata supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference reference supplied., No code repository or open-source implementation mentioned., No public dataset or benchmark details beyond IOI and induction described in the abstract., No statistical uncertainty, robustness tests, or detailed ablation study information in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-02 in cs.LG and cs.AI.; Introduces Conditional Co-Ablation as a label-free, output-grounded score.; Reports GPT-2-small IOI backup-head recovery improving from 0.33 to 0.91 ROC-AUC.; Claims CoAx outperforms self-repair-aware gradient baselines with best baseline ROC-AUC of 0.82.

TL;DR: The paper proposes Conditional Co-Ablation, a label-free intervention score for finding transformer circuit components that only become causally important after primary components are removed due to self-repair.

Problem: Mechanistic interpretability methods often score components by isolated ablation effects, which can miss backup components in transformer circuits because self-repair can mask the importance of both the primary component and its dormant substitute.

Method: The authors recast the issue as conditional circuit completion and introduce Conditional Co-Ablation (CoAx), which measures how much a remaining unit's ablation effect grows after a primary component set has already been removed.

Key contribution: The genuinely new idea is to score component importance conditionally under an intervention that makes backup components necessary, exposing second-order interactions that standard single-unit ablation and first-order attribution methods discard.

Main result: On the GPT-2-small IOI circuit, CoAx reportedly improves backup-head recovery from 0.33 to 0.91 ROC-AUC and outperforms baselines including self-repair-aware gradient scores with best reported ROC-AUC of 0.82; the abstract also claims transfer to induction across eight models and pruning experiments from 124M to 7B parameters.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code availability, affiliations, statistical uncertainty, detailed datasets, ablation studies beyond named baselines, or robustness analysis, so reproducibility and generality remain uncertain from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: This is highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it studies causal circuit discovery via interventions, counterfactual patching, ablation effects, and conditional causal importance in transformer mechanisms.

Tags: mechanistic-interpretability, causal-interventions, transformer-circuits, self-repair, ablation, GPT-2, IOI, induction-heads

Figure note: A useful image would show the CoAx workflow: remove a primary component set, measure conditional growth in ablation effects for remaining units, and recover backup heads in a transformer circuit; no direct figure URL is available from the supplied metadata.

Problem: The paper studies packet loss concealment for audio, where deployed models are usually fixed even though each lossy call or recording contains signal-specific information in the received packets.

Method: It proposes a self-supervised test-time tuning framework that synthetically masks available audio packets, trains the existing PLC model to reconstruct them with its native objective, and then uses the adapted parameters to conceal the true packet losses.

Key contribution: The main novelty is treating PLC models as adaptable at inference time without clean references, external adaptation data, architectural changes, or revising already emitted samples in the causal streaming setting.

Main result: From the abstract, the framework is instantiated on two public PLC backbones, FRN for full-band speech and PARCnet for networked music, and reportedly improves concealment by using observed portions of the same lossy signal as training data; no quantitative metrics are provided in the supplied abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide numerical gains, datasets, baselines beyond the two backbones, ablations, runtime cost, robustness evidence, code availability, or failure cases.

TL;DR: This paper uses a matched difference-in-differences design on GitHub projects to test whether adopting AI coding agents crowds out OSS newcomers, finding modest code-complexity increases but no significant decline in newcomer participation.

Problem: The paper studies whether AI coding agents in open-source software projects reduce newcomer inflow by taking over beginner-friendly tasks or making code harder to read.

Method: It identifies 1,888 GitHub projects that adopted an AI coding agent via first commits of agent configuration files, then applies difference-in-differences against matched non-adopting controls, with the main analysis restricted to 603 adopters that have a genuine pre-adoption period.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a causal empirical test of the feared trade-off between AI coding-agent adoption and human newcomer participation in OSS, separating the effect on code complexity from the effect on newcomer inflow, onboarding, and retention.

Main result: The abstract reports no evidence of newcomer crowding-out after AI agent adoption: newcomer inflow, onboarding, and retention do not significantly decline, while code complexity rises modestly, about +11% on a Python cognitive complexity metric and +3% to +4% in cyclomatic complexity across languages.

Limitation: The beginner-task measure based on good-first-issue labels is described as sparse and correlational, and the abstract says parallel trends cannot be tested for that measure; no code, data release, robustness appendix, affiliation, venue, DOI, or citation metadata is supplied.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query 'causality' because it frames the OSS AI-agent adoption question as a causal problem and uses matched difference-in-differences estimators to estimate adoption effects.

item.title: PARTREP: Learning What to Repeat for Decoder-only LLMs
item.description:
Priority: 53/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 50, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 76

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index signal, DOI, conference or journal venue, open-source code, public data or evaluation scripts

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-07-02; no DOI or journal reference supplied.; Abstract states evaluation on eight benchmarks and three model families.; Reports resource savings: 59.4% of full repetition KV cache and 79.0% of full repetition prefill FLOPs.; Comment says 15 pages and 7 figures including appendix.

TL;DR: PARTREP selectively repeats high-information prompt tokens in decoder-only LLMs to recover much of prompt-repetition's reasoning benefit while reducing long-context KV-cache and prefill costs.

Problem: The paper studies asymmetric information flow in decoder-only LLMs caused by causal attention, where later tokens are more contextually grounded than earlier tokens; full prompt repetition can help but is too expensive for long-context use.

Method: It proposes PartRep, which selects prompt tokens to repeat using token-wise negative log-likelihood as an informativeness signal, then trains a lightweight gate to predict high-NLL tokens from early-layer hidden states so selection can happen during mid-prefill via early exit.

Key contribution: The genuinely new idea is replacing full prompt repetition with learned partial repetition of less predictable, more context-critical tokens, reducing KV-cache and prefill overhead while preserving most of the performance gains from full repetition.

Main result: Across eight benchmarks including MMLU, GSM8K, and RULER, and three model families Qwen2.5, Llama3.2, and Gemma4, PartRep reportedly retains most gains of full repetition while using 59.4% of its KV cache and 79.0% of its prefill FLOPs.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide exact accuracy numbers, full baseline comparisons, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, gate-training cost, or details on whether gains hold across very different prompt styles and deployment settings.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it directly addresses causal attention in decoder-only transformers; it is relevant to LLM architecture and inference-efficiency work, but only tangentially relevant if the intended query meaning was causal inference or causality reasoning.

Tags: decoder-only LLMs, causal attention, prompt repetition, KV cache efficiency, prefill FLOPs, token selection, long-context inference, cs.CL

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or architecture figure showing normal prompt prefill, full prompt repetition, and PartRep's early-layer gate selecting high-NLL tokens for partial late-position repetition.

Authors: Andikawati P Widjaja, Yongjun Kim, Hyounghun Kim, Jaeho Lee

item.title: Set Diffusion: Interpolating Token Orderings Between Autoregression and Diffusion for Fast and Flexible Decoding
item.description:
Priority: 58/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 75, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 80, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations absent, citation counts absent, h-index absent, DOI absent, journal reference absent, specific datasets absent from abstract

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-07-02.; Comment says ICML 2026.; Abstract states code, model weights, and blog post are provided.; Metadata includes GitHub URL: https://github.com/kuleshov-group/setdlms.

TL;DR: The paper introduces set diffusion language models, a diffusion-style LM framework that factorizes generation over flexible token sets to support faster, more flexible decoding than fixed-block diffusion approaches.

Problem: Discrete diffusion language models are improving but are usually limited by fixed-length generation and lack KV caching, while block diffusion only partly addresses this by using fixed-size left-to-right token blocks that constrain decoding order, flexibility, and parallelism.

Method: The paper proposes set diffusion: a likelihood parameterization over flexible-position, flexible-length token sets plus a set-causal diffusion architecture that can update KV caches after every inference step. This allows arbitrary-order token-set decoding, including sliding-window sets.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is replacing fixed-size sequential diffusion blocks with flexible token sets, allowing interpolation between autoregressive and diffusion-style decoding while retaining KV-cache-compatible inference updates.

Main result: From the abstract, set diffusion reportedly achieves better speed-quality tradeoffs than prior diffusion language models on mathematical reasoning, summarization, and unconditional generation, and stronger infilling performance than block diffusion. Specific metrics, datasets, and effect sizes are not provided in the supplied abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative results, dataset names, baseline details, ablations, robustness tests, or failure cases, so empirical strength cannot be fully assessed from the supplied metadata alone.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because it uses a "set-causal diffusion architecture," but this appears to concern causal decoding/order constraints in language modeling rather than causal inference. Relevance is therefore moderate to low unless the feed is intended to include causal sequence modeling architectures.

Tags: discrete diffusion, language models, decoding, KV cache, non-autoregressive generation, block diffusion, set-causal architecture

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's framework diagram comparing autoregressive decoding, fixed block diffusion, and flexible token-set decoding with KV-cache updates.

Authors: Marianne Arriola, Volodymyr Kuleshov

Query: causality

item.title: Repair the Amplifier, Not the Symptom: Stable World-Model Correction for Agent Rollouts
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 32, Paper 38, Empirical 62, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: affiliations, author citation or h-index signals, DOI or venue acceptance, exact empirical metrics, datasets or benchmark descriptions, baseline implementation details beyond high-level description

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.AI, published 2026-07-02, comment says Under Review.; Abstract defines WM-SAR as World-Model Subgraph Amplification Repair.; Abstract compares WM-SAR against engineering LLM correctors that scan nodes and edges for suspicious local regions.; Abstract reports graph simulations and LLM repair experiments under realistic token budgets.

TL;DR: The paper proposes WM-SAR, a causal-subgraph repair method for correcting failed agent planning graphs without replaying or replanning the full workflow.

Problem: Persistent agent workflows may contain thousands of planning steps, so failures arise inside large planning graphs; the paper studies how to repair those graphs in place without expensive full-graph replanning or noisy long-context replay.

Method: WM-SAR works backward from subgraph amplification, identifies nodes and edges that repeatedly amplify an error, and sends only that causal subgraph to an LLM for repair; the paper compares this to engineering-style LLM correctors that scan graph regions for suspicious local symptoms.

Key contribution: The main novelty is framing graph repair around error amplification and causal subgraph selection rather than symptom-local scanning, aiming to give the LLM a smaller and cleaner repair target under token constraints.

Main result: The abstract reports that WM-SAR substantially outperforms engineering correctors across graph simulations and LLM repair experiments under realistic token budgets, and achieves near-whole-graph stabilization from a compact repair region; exact metrics are not provided in the supplied abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide numerical results, datasets, code availability, ablations, robustness tests, affiliations, citation signals, or enough detail to assess reproducibility independently.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because its repair mechanism explicitly identifies a causal subgraph responsible for re-amplifying errors in agent world-model planning graphs.

Tags: agent planning, world models, causal subgraph, LLM repair, workflow stabilization, graph correction

Figure note: A pipeline or architecture figure showing full planning graph failure, amplification-based causal subgraph extraction, and LLM repair of the compact subgraph would be useful, but no direct image URL is available from the supplied metadata.

item.title: InterCMDM: Block-Causal Diffusion for Autoregressive Human Interaction Generation
item.description:
Priority: 61/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 46, Paper 75, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 40, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Author citation counts or h-index not supplied., No DOI or journal reference supplied., No quantitative metrics in abstract., No baseline names, ablations, or robustness tests in abstract., No explicit code or dataset release statement in supplied metadata.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.CV, published 2026-07-02.; Comment states accepted to ECCV 2026 and provides a project website.; Abstract proposes InterCMDM with block-causal latent diffusion and a Dual-Stream Causal Diffusion Transformer.; Abstract claims state-of-the-art performance on InterHuman and Inter-X, but gives no numbers.

TL;DR: InterCMDM proposes a block-causal latent diffusion framework for text-conditioned two-person motion generation that preserves causal temporal rollout while modeling coordinated partner interactions.

Problem: The paper studies text-conditioned human interaction generation, where models must maintain long-range temporal causality for each individual while coordinating tightly coupled two-person behavior over long horizons.

Method: It proposes InterCMDM, a block-causal latent diffusion framework with a Dual-Stream Causal Diffusion Transformer. The model keeps separate causal streams for each person, uses unified dual-stream attention with multi-task attention masks to model inter-person dependencies, and applies a block-wise diffusion objective for stable autoregressive rollout.

Key contribution: The main novelty is combining block-causal autoregressive diffusion with configurable dual-stream attention masks that support simultaneous, reactive, leader-follower, and independent interaction modes in one model, enabling controllable coordination at inference by selecting an attention mask.

Main result: The abstract reports state-of-the-art performance on InterHuman and Inter-X, with improvements in text-motion alignment, realism, and long-horizon continuity, but does not provide quantitative numbers.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide metric values, baseline names, ablation evidence, robustness analysis, code availability, or details on failure cases; reproducibility and empirical strength therefore remain uncertain from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query "causality" because the central technical claim is causal, block-wise autoregressive diffusion for long-horizon human interaction generation, explicitly addressing bidirectional-attention diffusion and drift in autoregressive alternatives.

Tags: causality, human interaction generation, diffusion models, autoregressive generation, motion synthesis, text-to-motion, cs.CV

Figure note: A useful image would be an architecture or pipeline diagram showing the dual causal person streams, unified dual-stream attention masks, and block-wise latent diffusion rollout.

Method: It proposes TCA-Captioner, built around an Observer-Checker-Corrector iterative refinement strategy for producing grounded training data, trained on a curated high-density human interaction dataset, and evaluated with TCA-Bench using a decoupled protocol for audiovisual binding and temporal relational reasoning.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed in the abstract is the combination of OCC-based training-data refinement, an alignment-specialized captioning framework, and a diagnostic benchmark designed to isolate audiovisual binding and temporal reasoning rather than only scoring generic caption quality.

Main result: The abstract claims extensive experiments show TCA-Captioner sets a new standard for temporally coherent and synchronized audiovisual narratives, but it does not provide quantitative scores, baselines, datasets, or effect sizes.

item.title: The risk of KV cache compression
item.description:
Priority: 39/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 28, Paper 30, Empirical 45, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not provided., Citation count and h-index signals not provided., No DOI, journal, or conference venue provided., No code repository or implementation release stated., No quantitative LongBench results in abstract., No named baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, or detailed metrics in abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-01.; Abstract states the bottleneck is repeated reads from a large KV cache during long-sequence Transformer inference.; Claims a minimax-risk characterization based on intrinsic cache compressibility.; Claims design principles for causal masking that map to prefill and autoregressive decoding.

TL;DR: The paper develops a theoretical minimax-risk framework for KV cache compression in long-sequence Transformer inference and derives causal-masking-aware design principles with a practical algorithm tested in targeted LongBench experiments.

Problem: Transformer inference over long sequences is costly because softmax attention repeatedly accesses a large KV cache; existing KV cache compression methods are mostly empirically designed, while prior theory mainly gives worst-case impossibility results rather than constructive guidance.

Method: The paper characterizes the minimax risk of KV cache compression as a function of intrinsic cache compressibility, then uses this theory to derive design principles for compression under causal masking that can be implemented during prefill and autoregressive decoding. It instantiates these principles in a practical algorithm.

Key contribution: Its main novelty is a principled minimax-risk characterization that identifies when accurate KV cache compression is possible and translates that theory into causal-masking-compatible algorithmic guidance, rather than relying only on empirical cache-summary heuristics.

Main result: The abstract reports promising targeted LongBench performance and claims minimax-optimal risk, but does not provide specific metrics, baselines, or effect sizes.

Limitation: The abstract gives limited empirical detail: no quantitative LongBench scores, no named baselines, no ablations, no robustness analysis, and no code or data release information are stated.

Relevance: Relevant only tangentially to the matched query "causality": the abstract mentions causal masking in Transformer decoding, not causal inference or causal modeling. It is more directly relevant to long-context inference, attention efficiency, and KV cache compression.

Tags: KV cache compression, Transformer inference, long-context models, minimax risk, causal masking, LongBench

Figure note: A useful figure would show the minimax-risk framework and how the proposed compression algorithm operates across prefill and autoregressive decoding under causal masking.

Authors: Lukas Haverbeck, Carmen Amo Alonso, Andres Felipe Posada-Moreno, Sebastian Trimpe, Marco Pavone

item.title: How Much Future Helps? A Controlled Study of Future-Privileged Supervision for Causal Egocentric Gaze Estimation
item.description:
Priority: 61/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 48, Paper 72, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 38, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index signals, DOI or journal reference, open-source code, implementation details, exact metrics

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-01.; Accepted to GAZE 2026 at CVPR 2026 workshop and marked Best Paper Award in metadata.; Evaluates on EGTEA Gaze+ and Ego4D.; Reports optimal future look-ahead ranges rather than monotonic gains.

TL;DR: The paper studies whether future context can improve strictly causal online egocentric gaze estimation by using a training-only future-aware supervision branch with tunable look-ahead.

Problem: Egocentric gaze estimation is often evaluated with models that can process full videos and future frames, but real-world online systems require causal prediction without future access at inference.

Method: The authors propose a controlled framework with a causal inference model plus a future-aware branch that can access a configurable future look-ahead horizon during training; the future-aware branch is discarded at inference so prediction remains strictly causal.

Key contribution: It isolates the effect of future context on causal gaze prediction and quantifies the useful look-ahead range, showing that future-privileged supervision helps but longer future access is not always better.

Main result: Across EGTEA Gaze+ and Ego4D, future-privileged supervision consistently improves causal gaze prediction, with best performance around 1.7-3.3 seconds of future context on EGTEA Gaze+ and 2.7 seconds on Ego4D, according to the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not report exact metrics, baseline comparisons, ablations beyond the horizon study, statistical robustness, code availability, or whether the finding generalizes beyond the two named datasets.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the central question is how to train strictly causal online vision models using future information only during supervision, without violating causal inference constraints.

Tags: causal inference, egocentric vision, gaze estimation, future-privileged supervision, online prediction, cs.CV

Figure note: A framework diagram showing the causal inference branch, the training-only future-aware branch, and tunable look-ahead horizon H would be useful, but no direct image URL is supplied.

item.title: TurnNat: Automatic Evaluation of Turn-Taking Naturalness in Dyadic Spoken Dialogue
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 26, Paper 35, Empirical 60, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index signals, DOI or venue signal, citation count, open-source code, public benchmark or data release

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-01 with no DOI or journal reference in supplied metadata.; Abstract proposes TurnNat, a likelihood-based framework using a causal turn-taking prediction model and NLL of observed future activity.; Abstract reports a controlled perturbation benchmark validated by human naturalness judgments.; Abstract claims TurnNat identifies unnatural perturbations across heterogeneous timing failures.

TL;DR: TurnNat proposes a likelihood-based metric for evaluating turn-taking naturalness in two-channel spoken dialogue by scoring how atypical observed future voice-activity patterns are under a causal turn-taking prediction model trained on natural conversations.

Problem: The paper studies automatic evaluation of turn-taking naturalness in full-duplex spoken dialogue systems, where existing methods rely on human judgments or narrow timing metrics that do not compare heterogeneous timing failures in a unified way.

Method: It proposes TurnNat: a causal turn-taking prediction model estimates future two-speaker voice-activity states, frame-level negative log-likelihood scores measure timing atypicality, and scores are pooled over turn-taking boundary units from utterance onsets and offsets before being aggregated into dialogue-level naturalness scores.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is a unified likelihood-based evaluation framework that converts varied turn-taking timing failures into comparable NLL-based naturalness scores rather than evaluating only specific behaviors such as pauses, overlaps, or interruptions.

Main result: On a controlled perturbation benchmark of paired natural and perturbed dialogue clips validated by human naturalness judgments, TurnNat identifies unnatural turn-taking perturbations across heterogeneous timing failures.

Limitation: The abstract does not report benchmark size, datasets, quantitative metrics, baselines, ablations, robustness tests, open-source code, or whether the method generalizes beyond the constructed perturbation benchmark.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because it uses a causal prediction model, but its main contribution is dialogue naturalness evaluation rather than causal inference or causal discovery, so relevance depends on whether the feed is meant to include causal sequence-prediction methods.

Tags: spoken-dialogue, turn-taking, evaluation, likelihood-modeling, full-duplex-dialogue, causal-prediction

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline figure showing two-channel voice activity input, causal future-state prediction, frame-level NLL scoring, TBU pooling around utterance onsets/offsets, and dialogue-level aggregation.

TL;DR: The paper applies sparse autoencoders to neural quantum states to identify unsupervised internal features that correlate with and causally steer physical observables.

Problem: Neural Quantum States can accurately model quantum many-body wavefunctions, but their internal mechanisms and how they encode physical observables not directly optimized in the variational objective remain poorly understood.

Method: The authors analyze residual-stream activations of trained NQS using sparse autoencoders, identify learned features correlated with physical observables, and perform targeted post-training interventions on individual features to test causal effects on predicted observables.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that unsupervised SAE features inside NQS correspond to physical quantities and that steering a single learned feature can monotonically change the associated observable while leaving variational energy nearly unchanged.

Main result: From the abstract: SAE-derived features strongly correlate with order parameters, staggered magnetization, and half-chain correlators across ground-state representation and real-time dynamics, and single-feature interventions causally steer corresponding observables.

Limitation: The abstract does not report quantitative effect sizes, baselines against other interpretability methods, ablations, statistical robustness, code availability, or detailed dataset/model coverage.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query "causality" through causal feature steering and post-training interventions, with relevance to mechanistic interpretability, representation analysis, and causal control in learned scientific models.

TL;DR: TiRex-2 is a recurrent xLSTM-based time-series foundation model for multivariate, streaming forecasting with past and future covariates, claiming state-of-the-art zero-shot results while preserving target-variable causality and constant per-patch inference cost.

Problem: The paper studies multivariate time-series forecasting in streaming settings where observations arrive sequentially, variables evolve jointly, and some covariates are known in the future; it targets the quadratic context cost and full-history recomputation required by Transformer-based time-series foundation models.

Method: It proposes TiRex-2, a memory-centric recurrent xLSTM architecture with a bidirectional time mixer and an asymmetric grouped-attention variate mixer, plus a synthetic coupling pipeline that constructs multivariate pretraining samples from large univariate corpora.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is combining multivariate forecasting, streaming constant per-patch cost, future-known covariate integration, and strict causality over target variables in one time-series foundation model.

Main result: The abstract reports state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on GIFT-Eval and fev-bench, stability when streamed to arbitrary context lengths, and constant inference cost per patch.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative benchmark values, baseline names, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, or dataset/pretraining details beyond the synthetic coupling pipeline.

Relevance: The matched query is "causality"; relevance is moderate because the paper concerns architectural/forecasting causality, specifically strict causal handling of target variables, rather than causal inference or causal discovery.

TL;DR: DiscoPER is an LLM-powered autonomous discovery framework that performs open-ended dataset exploration, statistically tests generated hypotheses, and uses meta-reflection over prior discoveries to redirect search.

Problem: The paper studies how autonomous scientific discovery systems can move beyond constrained search spaces and predefined research questions toward open-ended hypothesis generation and validation.

Method: It proposes DiscoPER, a framework that dynamically generates and executes code to explore datasets, requires proposed discoveries to pass statistical testing, periodically performs second-order reasoning over accumulated discoveries, and uses tools to extract information from multimodal sources such as images.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element in the abstract is treating the system's own prior discoveries as empirical data for meta-reflection, allowing it to identify structural patterns, confounds, and gaps and redirect future hypothesis search.

Main result: On iNatDisco, a new multimodal ecological knowledge benchmark with pattern-level ground truth from peer-reviewed literature, DiscoPER reportedly recovers 8 of 9 known patterns with a 72.7% hypothesis support rate and outperforms classical causal discovery and LLM-guided baselines.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code or data availability, detailed baseline identities, statistical robustness beyond hypothesis testing, external-domain validation, or whether iNatDisco is public.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured causal discovery feed because it compares against classical causal discovery baselines and proposes an autonomous hypothesis-discovery workflow that explicitly searches for patterns, confounds, and statistically supported relationships.

item.title: CausalMix: Data Mixture as Causal Inference for Language Model Training
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

TL;DR: CausalMix frames LLM data-mixture optimization as a causal inference problem so mixture weights can adapt when the data pool changes, rather than requiring proxy-model retraining from scratch.

Problem: The paper studies how to optimize training-data mixtures for LLMs under shifting data pools, where prior proxy-model approaches assume static distributions and become costly when scaling to larger pools or models.

Method: It proposes CausalMix, which treats statistical features of the data pool as covariates and domain mixture as the treatment, fits a causal model from 512 Qwen2.5-0.5B runs to estimate CATE, and uses the inferred effects to choose mixtures for larger-scale training.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is recasting data mixture optimization as causal treatment-effect estimation, enabling state-dependent mixture extrapolation and interpretability through a CATE Interpreter, instead of learning fixed mixture weights for a static pool.

Main result: From the abstract, CausalMix extrapolates to an 800K data pool for 7B training, generalizes to long chain-of-thought data on Qwen3-4B-Base, and reportedly outperforms RegMix and other baselines across multiple downstream tasks.

Limitation: The abstract does not report exact metrics, dataset/task names, ablations, statistical robustness, code availability, public data availability, or evidence that the causal assumptions hold under all distribution shifts.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query because it explicitly applies causal inference, CATE estimation, and confounding-bias isolation to an LLM training problem.

Tags: causal inference, data mixture optimization, LLM training, CATE, Qwen, domain mixture, scaling data pools

Figure note: The metadata says the paper has 3 figures, and the abstract mentions a CATE Interpreter visualization; a framework diagram showing covariates, treatment/domain mixture, CATE estimation, and extrapolated mixture selection would be useful.

item.title: Reading Order Inference for Complex Document Layouts
item.description:
Priority: 45/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 14, Paper 30, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation counts or h-index, DOI, journal or conference acceptance, open-source code link, repository quality

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-01 with no DOI, journal reference, or conference venue in supplied metadata.; Method is described as training-free and graph-based, using causal LM likelihood and BERT NSP edge scores.; Evaluated on synthetic Glossa Ordinaria grid layouts, 23 ALTO page geometries, and a 140-page OmniDocBench multi-column English subset.; Compares against recursive XY-cut/PaddleOCR PP-StructureV3 and two LayoutReader variants.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a training-free graph-based method for inferring line-level reading order in complex historical and multi-column document layouts, using lightweight language-model edge scoring and max-regret path-cover inference.

Problem: It studies reading order inference for digitized documents with spatially interleaved text streams, especially historical Glossa Ordinaria-style layouts where central text and surrounding commentary form non-rectangular regions.

Method: Each OCR text line is represented as a node in a directed candidate-transition graph; candidate edges are scored with an additive ensemble of causal language-model conditional likelihood and BERT next-sentence prediction, then a degree-constrained directed path cover is recovered using a max-regret inference rule.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element described in the abstract is the training-free graph formulation with max-regret edge commitment to avoid greedy edge-selection failures in wrap-around layouts, rather than relying on recursive geometric cuts or learned layout readers.

Main result: On wrap-around Glossa layouts, the method recovers 95% of ground-truth successor edges versus 50% for XY-cut; on a 140-page OmniDocBench multi-column English subset it reports 88% macro edge accuracy versus 75% for XY-cut and 25% for LayoutReader.

item.title: LeNEPA: No-Augmentation Next-Latent Prediction for Time-Series Representation Learning
item.description:
Priority: 60/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 20, Paper 70, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 75, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-indexes, DOI or journal reference, statistical uncertainty across seeds, ablations, robustness tests

Score evidence: Accepted by KDD MILETS 2026 according to comment metadata.; Source code and artifacts URL is provided in the arXiv comment.; Evaluates PTB-XL, Diag, and an external UCR-128 frozen-encoder check.; Reports AUROC/AUPRC learning-curve gains and 77.65% mean UCR-128 Random-Forest accuracy.

TL;DR: LeNEPA proposes a no-augmentation next-latent prediction objective for time-series self-supervised learning, aiming to reduce dependence on domain-specific augmentations and retuning.

Problem: The paper studies whether a time-series SSL recipe remains useful when its method-specific configuration is reused unchanged after changing the pretraining signal family, framed as a fixed-recipe stress test rather than an optimally tuned benchmark comparison.

Method: It introduces Latent Euclidean Next-Embedding Prediction Architecture (LeNEPA), using a causal backbone, next-latent-token prediction, SIGReg-based isotropy regularization instead of stop-gradient/EMA stabilization, and a lightweight projected predictive-loss space discarded at evaluation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is a no-augmentation latent prediction recipe for time-series SSL that is explicitly evaluated for low-retuning transfer across datasets, contrasting with augmentation/view-dependent SSL recipes such as an ECG-tuned JEPA setup.

Main result: Under a fixed-horizon frozen-probe protocol on PTB-XL and Diag, LeNEPA preserves frozen-probe gains on both datasets while the ECG-tuned JEPA recipe is strong in-domain but weaker on Diag. LeNEPA reaches 80% of final AUROC/AUPRC gain after 2--5k updates versus 5--10k for the faster JEPA readout. A CauKer-pretrained LeNEPA variant reports 77.65% mean UCR-128 Random-Forest accuracy, close to Mantis and MOMENT in a single-seed best-checkpoint run.

Limitation: The abstract frames results as a fixed-recipe stress test, not a fully tuned comparison. The external UCR-128 check is single-seed and best-checkpoint. The abstract does not report ablations, statistical uncertainty, affiliation/citation metadata, DOI, or detailed repository quality.

Relevance: Matched query is "causality". Relevance appears moderate-to-weak: the paper uses a causal backbone for time-series representation learning, but the abstract does not indicate causal inference, causal discovery, interventions, or counterfactual modeling.

Tags: time-series, self-supervised-learning, representation-learning, latent-prediction, causal-backbone, no-augmentation, frozen-probe

Figure note: A useful image would be the LeNEPA architecture/pipeline figure showing the causal backbone, next-latent-token prediction target, SIGReg regularization, and discarded projected loss space.

TL;DR: Graph-PRefLexOR fine-tunes graph-native reasoning models with GRPO to make materials-science hypothesis generation more traceable by structuring reasoning into explicit graph-based phases.

Problem: The paper studies how AI systems can generate scientifically valid, traceable hypotheses for open-ended materials design problems, where standard LLMs may produce fluent answers without inspectable intermediate causal reasoning.

Method: It proposes Graph-PRefLexOR, a family of graph-native reasoning models fine-tuned with Group Relative Policy Optimization to organize reasoning into phases: mechanism exploration, graph construction, pattern extraction, and hypothesis synthesis.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is linking neural language generation with reusable symbolic relational graphs so causal connections can be constructed, inspected, and aligned with final scientific hypotheses.

Main result: On 100 open-ended materials science and mechanics questions, the abstract reports 40-65% improvements over corresponding base models, largest gains in reasoning traceability, and roughly 2-3x greater semantic diversity than baselines.

Limitation: The abstract does not identify the exact base models, scoring protocol, datasets/questions, code availability, statistical robustness, human evaluation details, or external validation of generated hypotheses.

item.title: Decoupled Guidance: Disentangling Subject and Context Pathways in Text-to-Image Personalization
item.description:
Priority: 46/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 29, Paper 45, Empirical 58, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 72

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-07-01; no DOI, journal, or conference metadata supplied.; Abstract names conditioning entanglement and links it to a fidelity-editability trade-off.; Abstract describes a causal intervention: replacing the target subject token with a generic subject token changes attention allocation and context adherence.; Abstract claims plug-and-play applicability without modifying backbone models.

TL;DR: DeGu is a plug-and-play text-to-image personalization framework that separates subject-identity and scene-context conditioning into independent guidance streams to reduce the fidelity-editability trade-off.

Problem: The paper studies conditioning entanglement in text-to-image personalization, where subject fidelity and prompt/context editability share the same conditioning pathway and compete for attention-map resources.

Method: It proposes Decoupled Guidance (DeGu), which routes subject identity and scene context through two independent guidance streams, then uses spatial mixing to dynamically fuse them in semantically relevant regions.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that fidelity-editability conflict is caused by conditioning entanglement, supported by a causal intervention replacing the target subject token with a generic subject token, and addressed through decoupled guidance without modifying backbone models.

Main result: The abstract reports that DeGu consistently improves overall personalization performance across diverse personalization methods and backbones, including flow-matching Diffusion Transformers, but gives no quantitative metrics.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide numerical gains, datasets, baselines, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, or human evaluation details, so the empirical strength cannot be fully assessed from supplied metadata.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the paper explicitly frames conditioning entanglement as a causal factor and reports causal evidence via token replacement interventions, though the main contribution is in text-to-image personalization rather than general causal inference.

Tags: text-to-image personalization, diffusion models, conditioning entanglement, causal intervention, guidance, DiT

Figure note: A useful image would be the framework or pipeline diagram showing the two independent guidance streams for subject identity and scene context plus the spatial mixing mechanism; no direct image URL is available from the supplied metadata.

item.title: DART: Difficulty-Adaptive Routing for Zero-Shot Video Temporal Grounding
item.description:
Priority: 63/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 40, Paper 78, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 76

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals not supplied., DOI and journal reference absent., Open-source code availability not stated in the abstract, despite a project homepage., Ablations, statistical robustness, and detailed failure analysis not visible in the abstract., Specific baseline names and full metric tables not supplied.

Score evidence: Accepted to ECCV 2026 according to the comment field.; arXiv version is v1, published 2026-07-01.; Reports evaluation on Charades-STA and ActivityNet Captions.; Claims up to 3.5 mIoU improvement over the strongest baseline and over 7x fewer frames.

TL;DR: DART is a zero-shot video temporal grounding framework that routes simple and complex language queries through fast prediction or structured LVLM reasoning to better localize multi-stage events requiring temporal and causal understanding.

Problem: The paper studies zero-shot video temporal grounding in untrimmed videos, focusing on the gap where frame-query feature matching handles simple events but struggles with complex multi-stage queries involving temporal ordering and causal structure.

Method: It proposes DART, which uses a query-conditioned Determinantal Point Process to select diverse query-relevant keyframes and estimate query difficulty via spectral entropy; simple queries use a Fast direct-prediction path, while complex queries use a Slow path with Temporal Markup Prompting for event analysis, frame role annotation, and boundary extraction.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is difficulty-adaptive routing coupled with structured LVLM reasoning, using the DPP both as temporal evidence selector and as a difficulty signal rather than applying the same frame-query matching pipeline to every query.

Main result: On Charades-STA and ActivityNet Captions, the abstract reports state-of-the-art zero-shot performance across identically distributed and multiple out-of-distribution settings, with up to 3.5 mIoU improvement over the strongest baseline while using more than 7x fewer frames.

Limitation: The abstract does not report ablations, statistical robustness, detailed baseline names, code availability, or failure cases; limitation discussion is not visible from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it explicitly targets complex VTG queries requiring temporal ordering and causal structure, although it appears primarily to be a vision-language grounding method rather than a general causal inference framework.

Tags: zero-shot video temporal grounding, vision-language models, temporal reasoning, causal structure, DPP, adaptive routing, ECCV 2026, cs.CV

Figure note: A pipeline figure showing DPP keyframe selection, spectral-entropy difficulty routing, and the Fast versus Slow Temporal Markup Prompting paths would be useful; no direct image URL is supplied.

item.title: Faithful by Definition: Emotion Analysis via Natural Semantic Metalanguage Explications
item.description:
Priority: 44/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 20, Paper 45, Empirical 45, Reproducibility 55, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation count or h-index metadata, DOI or venue signal, open-source code link, full baseline details, ablations

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-01 in cs.CL and cs.AI.; Method uses NSM explications with twelve typed slots and a fixed decision list from published semantic definitions.; Reports 0.33 parser accuracy and 0.48 selective accuracy on a small held-out set.; Claims release of EmoExpl-1200 with per-line verification metadata and full rule set.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a causally faithful emotion-analysis interface in which labels are computed from Natural Semantic Metalanguage explications rather than justified post hoc after black-box classification.

Problem: It studies the lack of faithfulness in post hoc explanations for emotion classifiers, especially for first-person event-based emotion analysis where explanations may not reflect the actual computation behind a predicted label.

Method: A learned parser maps input event descriptions into short Natural Semantic Metalanguage explications with twelve typed slots; a fixed decision list, transcribed from published semantic definitions, then assigns the emotion label using only that explication.

Key contribution: The novelty is a definitional faithfulness setup: the explanation is not a post hoc rationale but the causal decision basis itself, with empirical uncertainty isolated in the parser and made auditable through per-line entailment checks.

Main result: On crowd-sourced event descriptions, the fine-tuned parser reports 0.33 accuracy and 0.48 selective accuracy on a small held-out set, while the authors claim an insignificant accuracy difference versus a black-box model.

Limitation: The abstract reports a small held-out evaluation and low absolute accuracy; it does not provide enough detail on baselines, ablations, statistical tests, parser architecture, robustness, or external validation.

Relevance: The matched query is causality, and the paper directly frames explanation faithfulness as causal and definitional: labels are caused by explicit explication rules rather than opaque classifier internals.

Tags: causality, explainable-ai, emotion-analysis, natural-language-processing, natural-semantic-metalanguage, faithfulness, auditable-ml

Figure note: The comment says the paper has 8 figures, but no direct image URL is supplied; a pipeline figure showing input text, twelve-slot NSM explication, entailment audit, and rule-based label computation would be the most useful image.

item.title: Auditing Forgetting in Limited Memory Language Models
item.description:
Priority: 56/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 41, Paper 48, Empirical 80, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 83

Missing quality signals: No DOI, journal, or conference venue provided., No affiliation or author citation/h-index metadata provided., No code repository or data release mentioned., No explicit baselines beyond existing aggregate evaluation framing mentioned in the abstract., No ablation study or statistical robustness details stated in the abstract., No robustness evidence across multiple LMLM architectures stated.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-01.; Abstract defines three interventions: FULL, DEL-ON, and DEL-OFF.; Evaluation covers 12,228 alias-closure deletions across thirteen databases, four adversarial topologies, three domains, and six prompt formulations.; Reported parametric leakage is near zero, while residual correctness ranges from 0.7% to 13.6%.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a causal auditing framework for Limited Memory Language Models to distinguish whether deleted facts survive through parametric memory, retrieval-mediated reconstruction, or retrieval artifacts.

Problem: Existing LMLM unlearning evaluations report aggregate post-deletion correctness but do not identify the mechanism by which a deleted fact remains recoverable.

Method: The authors hold the model fixed and vary the database state at inference time using three interventions, FULL, DEL-ON, and DEL-OFF, then decompose behavior into parametric leakage, retrieval-mediated correctness, and retrieval artifact rate from retrieval traces.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a causal intervention-style audit that attributes post-deletion failures to retrieval graph effects rather than treating correctness after deletion as a single aggregate metric.

Main result: Across 12,228 alias-closure deletions, thirteen databases, four adversarial topologies, three domains, and six prompt formulations, parametric leakage is near zero; surviving deleted facts are mostly reconstituted through near-neighbor retrieval, ranging from 0.7% on the released LMLM database to 13.6% on the most adversarial variant.

Limitation: The abstract reports results for one class of LMLM and deletion procedure, but does not mention open-source code, public datasets, statistical uncertainty, broader model families, or external baselines.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it frames LMLM unlearning evaluation as a causal audit using controlled inference-time interventions.

Tags: causal auditing, unlearning, limited memory language models, retrieval, language models, knowledge deletion

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's causal auditing pipeline or framework figure showing the FULL, DEL-ON, and DEL-OFF database interventions and how they decompose leakage, retrieval-mediated correctness, and artifacts.

item.title: Pessimism's Paradox: Conservative Offline Training Amplifies Reward Hacking During Online Adaptation in Reasoning Models
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 39, Paper 55, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: No DOI or journal reference supplied., No affiliation or institution metadata supplied., No citation count or h-index metadata supplied., No code repository mentioned., No ablation study mentioned in the abstract., No statistical confidence intervals or robustness checks mentioned.

Score evidence: Uses Qwen3-14B policy trained with DPO at three conservatism levels.; Online adaptation is against a learned reward ensemble of 3 x Qwen3-1.7B models.; True performance is measured with GSM8K exact-answer accuracy.; Reports monotonic increase in Goodhart damage with higher conservatism and Spearman rho = 1.0 across three conditions.

TL;DR: The paper argues that stronger conservative offline DPO can paradoxically make reasoning-model policies more vulnerable to reward hacking during later online optimization against a learned reward model.

Problem: It studies whether conservative offline alignment is actually a safe starting point for online adaptation when the online objective is supplied by an imperfect learned reward model.

Method: The authors train a Qwen3-14B policy with DPO at three conservatism levels, then perform online adaptation against a learned reward ensemble of three Qwen3-1.7B models while measuring true GSM8K exact-answer accuracy, Goodhart gap, AUGC, entropy, response diversity, cosine distance, and ensemble disagreement.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is an empirical and mechanistic reversal of the usual intuition: more conservative offline training can compress entropy, reduce diversity, increase exploitable epistemic uncertainty, and thereby amplify reward hacking during online adaptation.

Main result: Higher offline conservatism monotonically increases reward-hacking damage across the three tested beta settings, with Spearman rho = 1.0 for beta versus AUGC, and the authors fit a power-law to identify a practical optimal beta rather than recommending maximal conservatism.

Limitation: The abstract reports only three conservatism conditions, one main base policy, one reasoning benchmark, and one reward-ensemble setup; it does not mention code release, ablations, robustness checks, additional datasets, statistical uncertainty, or broader model-family replication.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the paper explicitly frames a mechanistic three-link causal chain connecting offline DPO conservatism to entropy compression, reduced response diversity, increased reward-model uncertainty, and faster exploitation during online optimization.

Tags: reward hacking, offline-to-online adaptation, DPO, LLM alignment, reasoning models, Goodhart's law, mechanistic analysis, causality-adjacent

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's mechanism or pipeline figure showing high-beta DPO leading to entropy compression, reduced diversity, increased reward-ensemble disagreement, and amplified reward hacking during online adaptation.

item.description:

TL;DR: The paper studies whether causal drift mechanisms in sparse continuous-time stochastic diffusion systems can be recovered non-parametrically from one-time cross-sectional steady-state observations.

Problem: Recover the time-infinitesimal transition mechanism, specifically the diffusion drift that encodes causality, from observational cross-sectional data when trajectories are not observed.

Method: Assumes a time-homogeneous diffusion at equilibrium with known acyclic causal structure, proves non-parametric identifiability of the drift under a weak non-explosion criterion, and derives a consistent kernel estimator with cross-validation for hyperparameter tuning.

Key contribution: It claims full non-parametric recovery of a causal diffusion drift from steady-state observations, rather than requiring longitudinal or low-frequency trajectory data, under known-graph and equilibrium assumptions.

Main result: From the abstract: the authors prove identifiability of the full drift function and consistency of a non-parametric kernel estimator; simulations illustrate estimator behavior, but quantitative empirical results are not provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract relies on strong structural assumptions: known causal graph, acyclicity, time-homogeneous diffusion, equilibrium observations, and a non-explosion condition. It does not report baselines, ablations, robustness checks, public code, or real-data results.

Relevance: Directly relevant to the matched query `causality` because it proposes theory and estimation for recovering causal mechanisms in continuous-time stochastic systems from observational data, with motivation from gene expression analysis.

TL;DR: CouCE proposes a causal debiasing framework for deep metric learning that targets both background spurious correlations and foreground nuisance perturbations to improve zero-shot generalization.

Problem: The paper studies shortcut learning in Deep Metric Learning, arguing that standard objectives capture co-occurrence rather than causal similarity and therefore generalize poorly in zero-shot settings.

Method: It proposes Counterfactual Causal Embedding (CouCE), combining Orthogonal Dictionary-Based Backdoor Adjustment to isolate background confounders with Multi-Scale Randomized Causal Intervention to enforce invariance to foreground nuisances via Fourier amplitude randomization and symmetric KL regularization.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is a unified causal framework that addresses two structurally different confounder pathways simultaneously, while remaining compatible with proxy-based losses and requiring no inference-time architecture changes.

Main result: The abstract reports state-of-the-art performance on CUB-200-2011, Cars-196, and Stanford Online Products, but does not provide numeric gains, baselines, or statistical evidence.

Limitation: Evidence is limited to abstract-level claims; no code, ablations, robustness analysis, quantitative results, affiliations, DOI, or peer-review venue are provided in the supplied metadata.

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-06-29, category cs.RO.; Comment says 20 pages, 10 figures, submitted to IEEE Transactions on Robotics.; Abstract reports evaluation on 16 simulated tasks and 4 real-world experiments.; Abstract reports RMBench success of 73.6%, +62.4 percentage points over pi0.5 and +22.8 points over Mem-0.

TL;DR: Chronos reframes robot manipulation policies as non-Markovian dynamical systems whose latent state is the full observation-proprioception history, improving memory-dependent long-horizon manipulation in simulation and real-world tasks.

Problem: The paper studies failures of Markovian or short-context VLA and generative imitation policies in manipulation settings where identical current observations require different actions depending on prior task history.

Method: It proposes Chronos, a physics-informed full-history policy framework: each control step is represented by a fused observation/proprioception token aligned with physical time; a selective state space model propagates the causal historical state; IMLE produces a multimodal coarse action prior; and a second-order Schrodinger-inspired bridge refines actions by predicting acceleration fields.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed in the abstract is treating the full observation history as the latent state of policy dynamics rather than as auxiliary context, combining state-space memory with a physics-inspired action refinement mechanism for smoother long-horizon robot control.

Main result: Across 16 simulated tasks and 4 real-world experiments, Chronos reportedly achieves 73.6% average success on RMBench, outperforming pi0.5 by 62.4 percentage points and Mem-0 by 22.8 points while using far fewer parameters; in real-world dual-arm tasks with a single RGB camera it achieves 78% average success versus 7% for pi0.5.

Limitation: The abstract does not report ablations, code availability, statistical uncertainty, dataset release details, robustness tests, affiliations, citations, or independent validation; results are therefore promising but hard to assess for reproducibility and breadth from the supplied metadata alone.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because Chronos explicitly models a causal historical state for policy dynamics, although the work is mainly about robotics memory and non-Markovian control rather than causal inference theory.

Tags: robotics, long-horizon manipulation, non-Markovian policy, state space model, vision-language-action, causal history, imitation learning

Figure note: A useful image would be the Chronos pipeline figure showing history-token construction, selective state-space propagation, IMLE coarse action prior, and Schrodinger-inspired bridge refinement.

item.title: X-Morph: Human Motion Priors for Scalable Robot Learning Across Morphologies
item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.RO, published 2026-06-29.; Abstract describes X-Morph as a human-motion-to-robot-behavior pipeline.; Pipeline includes cross-morphology retargeting, privileged RL tracking, and distillation into a causal student policy.; Evaluation is claimed on a quadruped, a hexapod, and a quadruped with a manipulator.

TL;DR: X-Morph proposes a pipeline for converting human motion into deployable locomotion and loco-manipulation policies for non-humanoid legged robots, evaluated on quadruped, hexapod, and quadruped-manipulator platforms.

Problem: The paper studies how to reuse abundant human motion data for non-humanoid legged robots, where direct motion retargeting can look plausible but fail to be physically consistent or trackable under robot dynamics.

Method: X-Morph uses cross-morphology retargeting to produce kinematically plausible, intent-preserving robot references, then trains a privileged RL tracking policy and distills it into a causal student policy for deployment.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed in the abstract is an end-to-end human-motion-to-robot-behavior pipeline spanning multiple non-humanoid morphologies rather than focusing on humanoid retargeting or a single robot body.

Main result: The abstract reports evaluation on three distinct platforms and claims the learned policies track diverse retargeted motions, generalize to unseen human motions, and support video-based teleoperation, behavior-prior control, and text-conditioned motion generation; no quantitative metrics are provided in the supplied abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention baselines, ablations, quantitative tracking metrics, real-robot deployment evidence, robustness tests, code release, public datasets, or statistical evaluation.

Relevance: The match to the configured query 'causality' appears weak: the abstract uses 'causal student policy' in the sense of deployable non-privileged policy inference, not causal inference or causal modeling. It may still be tangentially relevant for researchers tracking policy distillation or robotics deployment pipelines.

Tags: robot-learning, motion-retargeting, reinforcement-learning, legged-robots, policy-distillation, weak-causality-match

item.title: Be Faithful When Response: Returning Fluent and Grounded Answers for Vision-Language Models Reinforcement Learning
item.description:
Priority: 36/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 22, Paper 25, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 60

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-29 in cs.AI.; Method uses six general VQA benchmarks to construct FaithfulQA.; A VLM-based judge is used to purify the dataset for causal consistency and visual faithfulness.; Abstract claims better answer accuracy, RL stability, and fewer visually unsupported reasoning traces.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Faithful Warm-Start strategy for vision-language model RL that pretrains on visually causal VQA-style supervision before sparse-reward reinforcement learning to reduce fluent but visually unsupported reasoning.

Problem: Direct RL for multimodal reasoning in VLMs can become unstable and exploit language priors, producing reasoning traces that sound plausible but are not grounded in the image.

Method: The authors curate FaithfulQA from six general VQA benchmarks by adding structured visual observations, question requirements, commonsense knowledge, domain knowledge, and final answers, then use a VLM-based judge to filter for causal consistency and visual faithfulness before RL optimization with sparse answer-level rewards.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element in the abstract is the warm-start framing: steer the policy into a visually faithful, causally grounded reasoning regime before RL, rather than relying on RL alone to discover grounded multimodal reasoning.

Main result: The abstract claims improved answer accuracy, more stable RL training, and reduced visually unsupported reasoning, but it provides no numerical results, benchmark table, or effect sizes.

Limitation: The abstract does not report exact metrics, baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, model sizes, implementation details, code release, or whether FaithfulQA is public.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the method explicitly constructs and filters vision-language samples for causal consistency and grounded image-question-answer relationships.

Tags: vision-language models, reinforcement learning, causal reasoning, visual grounding, VQA, dataset curation

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline figure showing FaithfulQA construction, VLM-judge filtering, faithful warm-start training, and subsequent sparse-reward RL optimization.

item.title: SAGA: Scene-Aware, Goal-Evolving Agents for Long-Horizon CivRealm Strategy Planning
item.description:
Priority: 58/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 32, Paper 55, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 62, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations absent, Citation and h-index signals absent, DOI, conference, or journal venue absent, Exact baseline names and numerical results absent from abstract, Statistical test details absent, Robustness and transfer beyond FreeCiv absent

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-29 in cs.AI.; Abstract states evaluation on FreeCiv with highest mean civilization score and lower variance than two strongest baselines.; Abstract reports 27% lower output tokens and ablation studies for each architectural component.; Comment lists code URL: https://github.com/KazeCloud/SAGA-Civrealm.

TL;DR: SAGA is an LLM multi-agent framework for long-horizon FreeCiv planning that uses semantic scene graphs, tool-queried specialist controllers, and causal cross-game feedback to improve strategic performance while reducing token cost.

Problem: The paper studies long-horizon strategic planning in complex strategy games under imperfect information and sparse rewards, where LLM agents struggle with spatial scene understanding, excessive monolithic context, coupled decision domains, and weak learning across episodes.

Method: It proposes SAGA, a multi-agent architecture with a Map-Semantic Scene Graph for typed spatial entity relations, a Tool-Augmented Planner that retrieves domain state on demand and delegates to specialist controllers, and a Dual-Horizon Feedback Loop combining within-game goal generation with structured cross-game causal post-mortems.

Key contribution: The main novelty is the combination of scene-aware spatial abstraction, tool-mediated domain decomposition, and cross-game causal strategic evolution, targeting three specific failure modes of standard LLM game agents rather than relying on raw state dumps or single-agent prompting.

Main result: From the abstract, SAGA achieves the highest mean civilization score on FreeCiv, lower variance than the two strongest baselines, significant gains on infrastructure construction, 27% lower output-token cost, and the highest end-of-chain score across five successive episodes; ablations reportedly show each component contributes independently.

Limitation: The abstract only reports results on FreeCiv and does not provide exact baseline names, effect sizes, statistical test details, robustness settings, repository quality, or evidence that the causal post-mortem mechanism transfers beyond this game environment.

Relevance: The match to the query 'causality' is focused but indirect: the paper uses structured cross-game causal post-mortems for strategic evolution, not general-purpose causal inference or causal discovery.

Tags: LLM agents, multi-agent planning, strategy games, FreeCiv, scene graph, tool use, causal feedback, long-horizon planning

Figure note: A useful image would be the framework or pipeline figure showing how the Map-Semantic Scene Graph, Tool-Augmented Planner, specialist controllers, and Dual-Horizon Feedback Loop interact during and across FreeCiv episodes.

item.title: Early Warning Signals for OpenVLA Failure under Visual Distribution Shift
item.description:
Priority: 53/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 20, Paper 40, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index signals, DOI, journal or conference venue, open-source code, public rollout data

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-29; no DOI or journal reference in supplied metadata.; Uses OpenVLA and LIBERO manipulation rollouts with the policy fixed throughout.; Occlusion stress test uses 100 episodes per condition and drops success from 57% to 17%.; Reports AUROC 0.972 and AUPRC 0.352 for a layer-16 logistic probe predicting failure within 15 steps.

TL;DR: The paper tests whether fixed OpenVLA internal activations can be used as early warning signals for near-term task failure under visual distribution shift in LIBERO manipulation rollouts.

Problem: Vision Language Action policies can fail when visual conditions shift, and those failures are hard to diagnose during execution.

Method: The authors keep OpenVLA fixed, log feedforward activations during rollouts, and train lightweight post-hoc monitors, especially logistic probes, to predict failure within a 15-step horizon under controlled visual shifts.

Key contribution: The main novelty is showing that failure-relevant information is linearly decodable from specific OpenVLA layers, with a layer-depth analysis and stress tests distinguishing occlusion-driven failure prediction from simpler visual-shift detection.

Main result: Occlusion reduces OpenVLA success from 57% to 17% over 100 episodes per condition; a layer-16 logistic probe reaches AUROC 0.972 and AUPRC 0.352 for 15-step failure prediction, outperforming a mean-difference direction and an action-disagreement baseline.

Limitation: The abstract explicitly says the experiments do not establish a causal mechanism, task held-out generalization, or a deployable recovery system; code, data, affiliations, DOI, and broader robustness evidence are not provided in the metadata.

Relevance: Matched the query 'causality', but relevance is mostly indirect: the paper studies diagnostic signals inside a VLA model and explicitly notes that it does not establish causality.

Tags: vision-language-action, OpenVLA, failure prediction, distribution shift, robot manipulation, linear probes, LIBERO, causality-limited

Figure note: The metadata says the paper has 1 figure, but no direct image URL is supplied; a useful image would be the monitoring pipeline showing OpenVLA rollout activations, layer probes, visual-shift conditions, and near-term failure labels.

item.title: Sample-Efficient Learning of Probabilistic Causes for Reachability in Markov Decision Processes with Probabilistic Guarantees
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 30, Paper 75, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals not supplied., DOI, journal reference, and external citation metadata not supplied., Open-source code or repository not mentioned., Public datasets or benchmark details not specified., Baselines, ablations, confidence intervals, and robustness checks not described in the abstract.

Score evidence: Accepted to UAI2026 as oral presentation.; arXiv version is v1, published 2026-06-29.; Abstract states correctness proofs and sample-complexity bounds.; Abstract describes an anytime learning-and-checking algorithm based on two-sided value iteration.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a sample-efficient learning framework for identifying probability-raising causes of reachability outcomes in unknown Markov decision processes with probabilistic guarantees.

Problem: Probabilistic model checking can quantify reachability risk in MDPs but gives limited explanation of why bad outcomes occur; existing probability-raising cause identification methods are poorly suited to unknown MDPs because they depend on hard-to-estimate probability gaps and require reachability values of the original MDP.

Method: The authors introduce a restart-based MDP modification that reduces PR-cause checking to two conditional reachability queries, then build an anytime learning-and-checking algorithm using two-sided value iteration to classify states as causal, non-causal, or undecided.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is a learning-compatible PR-causality reduction that avoids needing original-MDP reachability probabilities, together with correctness proofs, sample-complexity bounds, and an anytime classifier for unknown transition models.

Main result: The abstract reports experiments on two benchmarks showing reliable and fast identification of PR causes; no detailed metrics, baselines, or statistical robustness results are provided in the supplied abstract.

Limitation: Evidence is limited in the abstract: experiments are only described as two benchmarks, with no reported quantitative results, baseline comparisons, ablations, code availability, public data, or robustness analysis.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it studies probability-raising causality in MDPs, specifically causal state identification for reachability outcomes under uncertainty.

Tags: causality, probability-raising causality, Markov decision processes, probabilistic model checking, reinforcement learning, reachability, sample complexity, formal methods

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram showing the restart-based MDP modification, the two conditional reachability queries, and the anytime state-classification loop.
