item.title: Mechanistically Eliciting Latent Behaviors in Language Models
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 16, Paper 35, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, code release, data release, full baseline list

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-28 with no DOI, journal reference, or comment supplied.; Introduces Causal Perturbative Elicitation, an unsupervised tensor-decomposition-based method for discovering interpretable LoRAs.; Reports Countdown performance of 85% for CPE versus 87% for matched-wall-clock-time GRPO on Qwen3-8B.; Reports restoring 85% of locked BigCodeBench performance on a password-locked Llama3-70B setup.

TL;DR: The paper proposes Causal Perturbative Elicitation, an unsupervised method for discovering interpretable LoRA perturbations that elicit latent behaviors and hidden failure modes in large language models.

Problem: The paper studies how to discover diverse, generalizable perturbations of LLM internal computations that reveal hidden behavioral modes, including safety-relevant failure modes such as sandbagging, alignment faking, and reward hacking.

Method: It introduces Causal Perturbative Elicitation (CPE), an unsupervised framework that decomposes computations in a deep transformer slice with a heuristic tensor-decomposition-based algorithm to produce interpretable low-rank adapters. These LoRAs are then used to perturb model weights and elicit latent behaviors.

Key contribution: The novel claim is that CPE can learn many interpretable LoRA perturbations from a single example and explore behavior in weight space rather than token space, enabling unsupervised elicitation of complex and hidden behaviors without task-specific supervised training.

Main result: From the abstract: CPE matches GRPO on Countdown for Qwen3-8B under matched wall-clock time, achieving 85% versus 87%; restores 85% of locked BigCodeBench performance in a password-locked Llama3-70B setup; virtually eliminates alignment-faking behavior in a Llama3-70B-based model organism; and can initialize GPT-OSS-20B in an aligned basin for GRPO in a reward-hacking-prone environment.

Limitation: The abstract reports strong empirical claims but does not mention code release, ablations, statistical uncertainty, robustness checks, full baseline coverage, affiliations, citation signals, or peer-review status. The tensor-decomposition procedure is described as heuristic, and the abstract does not clarify when CPE fails or how sensitive it is to model, layer, or example choice.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query 'causality' through its causal perturbation framing and its attempt to mechanistically intervene on transformer internals to elicit behaviors.

Tags: LLM safety, mechanistic interpretability, causal interventions, LoRA, unsupervised elicitation, latent behaviors, alignment, reward hacking

Figure note: A useful figure would show the CPE pipeline: selecting a transformer slice, applying tensor decomposition, constructing interpretable LoRA perturbations, and evaluating elicited latent behaviors across safety and reasoning tasks.

item.title: Vision-Default, Prior-Override: Causal Mechanisms of Perception-Knowledge Conflict in Vision-Language Models
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 20, Paper 45, Empirical 76, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation and h-index metadata not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference reference supplied., No code repository or data availability mentioned., Exact VLM families and datasets are not named in the abstract., No ablation robustness statistics or confidence intervals described in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-26.; Abstract reports experiments across three VLM families.; Uses activation patching, component ablations, and mechanistic analysis.; Reports sparse causal heads: 2.5-4.8% of attention heads.

TL;DR: This paper studies how VLMs causally resolve conflicts between visual input and memorized world knowledge, identifying a sparse set of attention heads that enable prior-knowledge override.

Problem: Vision-language models may answer from visual evidence or from memorized world knowledge when the two disagree; the paper asks which internal components causally determine that choice.

Method: The authors combine activation patching at residual-stream, attention-head, and MLP-sublayer granularities with component ablations and mechanistic analysis across three VLM families.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a component-level causal account: prior-knowledge grounding depends on a small fraction of attention heads, while visual grounding appears to be the default behavior.

Main result: Only 2.5-4.8% of attention heads, concentrated in the second half of the network, are reported as causally necessary for prior grounding; ablating them flips 68-96% of prior-knowledge-prompt predictions to visually grounded answers while altering only 0.8-7.5% of visually grounded predictions.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, exact VLM families, code availability, statistical uncertainty, or whether the causal circuit generalizes beyond the tested conflict prompts.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the query 'causality' because it uses activation patching and ablation to identify causal mechanisms inside VLMs, rather than only measuring behavioral correlations.

Tags: vision-language models, mechanistic interpretability, causal analysis, activation patching, attention heads, knowledge conflict

Figure note: A useful figure would show the proposed sparse causal circuit: visual/default path, prior-override attention heads in later layers, and the routing-head versus writing-head decomposition.

item.title: Disentangling Continuous-Time Latent Dynamics: Identifiability of Latent SDEs via Diffusion Shifts
item.description:
Priority: 42/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 20, Paper 45, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI or venue signal, citation or influential-citation data, open-source code, public dataset details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-06-26 in cs.LG and stat.ML.; Studies additive-noise latent SDEs observed through an unknown nonlinear diffeomorphism.; Claims identifiability from two diagonal diffusion regimes with distinct coordinate-wise variance ratios.; Reports synthetic experiments and an application to Hardanger Bridge monitoring data.

TL;DR: The paper studies identifiability in continuous-time latent SDE causal representation learning and shows that environment-specific diagonal diffusion shifts can recover latent coordinates and, under smoothness assumptions, the instantaneous drift-Jacobian causal graph.

Problem: Discrete-time latent causal models have strong identifiability results, but identifiability for continuous-time latent stochastic differential equation models observed through unknown nonlinear transformations remains largely unresolved.

Method: The authors analyze additive-noise latent SDEs with shared drift and environment-specific diffusion covariance, proving identifiability from two diagonal diffusion regimes with distinct coordinate-wise variance ratios; they propose a two-stage estimator for latent disentanglement with optional graph recovery.

Key contribution: The main novelty is using environment-induced diffusion covariance shifts, rather than drift sparsity, to identify latent coordinates up to permutation and scaling in continuous-time latent SDEs, first for linear Ornstein-Uhlenbeck systems and then for general additive-noise latent SDEs.

Main result: The abstract reports theoretical identifiability under two diagonal diffusion regimes and synthetic experiments confirming the predicted identifiability boundary, plus an illustrative real-data application to Hardanger Bridge monitoring trajectories.

Limitation: Evidence is limited from the abstract: no baselines, ablations, robustness checks, code availability, dataset details, or quantitative metrics are reported; assumptions such as diagonal diffusion regimes, distinct variance ratios, additive noise, diffeomorphic observation map, and mild smoothness may restrict applicability.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query "causal representation learning" because it targets identifiability of latent causal variables and causal graphs in continuous-time time-series settings.

Tags: causal representation learning, latent SDE, continuous-time dynamics, identifiability, diffusion shifts, time series, causal discovery

Figure note: A useful figure would show the proposed two-stage pipeline: nonlinear observations across environments, diffusion-shift-based latent disentanglement, and optional drift-Jacobian graph recovery.

item.title: Estimation--Prediction Tradeoff in Causal Probabilistic Temporal Graphs
item.description:
Priority: 47/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 42, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliation, author citation or h-index evidence, DOI, journal or conference venue, open-source code, public data

Score evidence: Abstract explicitly studies temporal link prediction in probabilistic temporal graphs.; Proposes a probabilistic causal framework with transient edges and known ground-truth causal structure.; Derives the Cramer-Rao bound for a binary logistic parametrization.; Claims validation of a tradeoff between parameter estimation error and irreducible predictive loss.

TL;DR: The paper argues that temporal link prediction accuracy can be misleading in probabilistic causal temporal graphs because regimes that make causal parameters easier to estimate can simultaneously make individual edge predictions intrinsically uncertain.

Problem: It studies how temporal link prediction benchmarks can conflate reducible model error with irreducible stochastic uncertainty, especially when the goal is to learn an underlying causal mechanism rather than only predict unseen edges.

Method: The paper proposes a probabilistic causal framework for generating temporal graphs with transient edges and known ground-truth causal structure, analyzes a binary logistic parametrization, derives a Cramer-Rao bound, and validates an estimation-prediction tradeoff between parameter recovery and predictive loss.

Key contribution: Its main new claim is that Fisher-information-optimal regimes for recovering causal parameters can be high-entropy regimes where edge-level prediction is intrinsically harder, so predictive accuracy alone is not a reliable proxy for causal mechanism learning.

Main result: From the abstract, the main result is a theoretical and empirical validation of the tradeoff: improved parameter recoverability can coincide with higher irreducible predictive loss, motivating benchmarks that separate model error from process uncertainty.

Limitation: The abstract does not report dataset scale, baselines, ablations, code availability, or robustness checks; the comment labels the work preliminary, so empirical support appears limited from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it explicitly evaluates causal parameter recovery in temporal graph generation and challenges standard temporal link prediction evaluation through a causal-mechanism lens.

Tags: causality, temporal graphs, link prediction, probabilistic modeling, Cramer-Rao bound, Fisher information, benchmarking

Figure note: The metadata says the paper has 4 figures, but no direct image URL is supplied. A useful image would likely be the framework or pipeline figure showing the causal probabilistic temporal graph generator and how prediction loss is separated from causal parameter recovery.

item.title: Robust Harmful Features Under Jailbreak Attacks: Mechanistic Evidence from Attention Head Specialization in Large Language Models
item.description:
Priority: 60/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 26, Paper 85, Empirical 75, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: author affiliations absent, citation and h-index metadata absent, DOI absent, journal reference absent, code repository not mentioned, public data not mentioned

Score evidence: Accepted at ICML 2026 as an Oral presentation, per supplied comment.; Abstract identifies two head types: ACHs in early layers and SAHs in mid-layers.; Abstract reports ablation studies supporting causal roles for ACHs and SAHs.; Abstract reports token-level attribution implicating attack-template tokens in ACH suppression.

TL;DR: The paper argues that jailbreaks bypass refusal behavior by selectively suppressing early-layer adversarially compromised attention heads while leaving mid-layer safety-aligned activations intact, and that these persistent activations can be used for robust jailbreak detection without training.

Problem: The paper studies why jailbreak attacks can bypass LLM safety alignment despite internal safety features, focusing on the mechanistic role of attention heads in refusal failure and harmful-content representation.

Method: It proposes a mechanistic analysis of attention-head specialization, distinguishing Adversarially Compromised Heads in early layers from Safety-Aligned Heads in mid-layers, supported by ablations, token-level attribution, and activation-reading experiments for detection.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is the Robust Harmful Features phenomenon: successful jailbreaks do not erase all safety signals, but suppress specific early attention heads while mid-layer safety-aligned activations persist and remain useful for adversarially robust detection.

Main result: From the abstract: suppressing a small number of ACHs can induce jailbreak-like behavior on normally refused inputs; removing SAHs weakens mid-layer safety activations; reading persistent activations without training gives competitive aggregate detection performance with strong adversarial robustness.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify models, datasets, detection metrics, baseline systems, code availability, or robustness protocol details, so the strength and generality of the empirical evidence cannot be fully assessed from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: This is relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the paper explicitly uses ablation evidence to argue for causal roles of specific attention-head groups in jailbreak behavior and safety activation robustness.

Tags: LLM safety, jailbreaks, mechanistic interpretability, attention heads, causal ablation, adversarial robustness, safety detection

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or mechanistic pipeline figure showing early-layer ACH suppression, mid-layer SAH persistence, token-level attribution from attack-template tokens, and the no-training activation-reading detector.

item.title: MultiHashFormer: Hash-based Generative Language Models
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 20, Paper 35, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation count or h-index, DOI or accepted venue, code repository, public data details, benchmark names

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-06-26, comment says under review.; Abstract states prior hash approaches collide many tokens into one vector and are unsuitable for causal LMs.; Abstract claims evaluation at 100M, 1B, and 3B parameter scales across multiple benchmarks.; Abstract claims constant-parameter multilingual vocabulary expansion without model modifications.

TL;DR: MultiHashFormer proposes a hash-signature token representation that makes hash-based embeddings usable for autoregressive language modeling while keeping vocabulary expansion parameter-efficient.

Problem: The paper studies how to reduce the parameter footprint of language-model embedding matrices, which grow linearly with vocabulary size, without losing the ability to perform causal autoregressive generation.

Method: Each token is assigned a unique short sequence of discrete hash IDs produced by multiple independent hash functions; a Hash Encoder compresses this signature into a latent vector for a Transformer decoder, and a Hash Decoder predicts the next token's hash signature before mapping it back to text.

Key contribution: The novel claim is enabling hash-based token compression for causal LMs by avoiding many-to-one token collisions through unique multi-hash signatures, whereas prior hash-embedding work was limited to encoder-only settings.

Main result: From the abstract: MultiHashFormer is evaluated at 100M, 1B, and 3B parameter scales and is reported to consistently outperform standard Transformer LMs across multiple benchmarks; it also supports multilingual vocabulary expansion with constant parameter footprint.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide benchmark names, metric values, baseline details, ablations, code availability, or robustness evidence; the paper is listed as under review with no DOI or venue metadata.

Relevance: Only weakly relevant to the matched query 'causality': the abstract uses 'causal LMs' in the autoregressive language-modeling sense, not causality or causal inference.

Tags: language-models, hash-embeddings, autoregressive-generation, parameter-efficiency, vocabulary-expansion, cs.CL

Figure note: A useful figure would show the MultiHashFormer pipeline: token to multi-hash signature, Hash Encoder to latent vector, Transformer decoder processing, Hash Decoder prediction of next-token hash signature, and mapping back to text.

item.title: Lifted Causal Inference
item.description:
Priority: 46/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 25, Paper 62, Empirical 45, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI, journal reference details, open-source code, public data

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-06-26.; Comment says accepted to the Annals of Mathematics and Artificial Intelligence journal.; Introduces PCFGs, PD-PCFGs, formal intervention semantics, and LCI.; Abstract claims drastic speedups compared to propositional inference but gives no quantitative result.

TL;DR: The paper introduces parametric causal factor graphs and a Lifted Causal Inference algorithm for computing causal effects efficiently in relational probabilistic models, including a partially directed extension for incomplete causal knowledge.

Problem: The paper studies how to compute causal effects in relational domains without fully grounding objects into propositional causal models, where standard propositional inference can be inefficient.

Method: It proposes parametric causal factor graphs (PCFGs) with formal intervention semantics, a Lifted Causal Inference (LCI) algorithm that performs causal inference at the lifted level, and partially directed PCFGs (PD-PCFGs) for settings with partial causal knowledge.

Key contribution: The new contribution is combining lifted probabilistic inference with causal-effect computation, including a formal causal semantics for lifted relational factor-graph models and an extension to partially directed causal structure.

Main result: The abstract claims LCI can drastically speed up causal inference compared with propositional inference such as causal Bayesian networks, but it does not provide quantitative speedups, datasets, or metrics.

Limitation: The abstract does not report concrete empirical results, implementation availability, datasets, ablations, robustness checks, or detailed assumptions required for correctness beyond the formal framework claim.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query "causal inference" because it directly targets causal-effect computation and intervention semantics, especially for relational or lifted probabilistic graphical models.

Tags: causal inference, lifted inference, probabilistic graphical models, relational models, interventions, factor graphs

item.title: A Toolkit for the Study of Treatment-Effect Discontinuities
item.description:
Priority: 47/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 50, Paper 35, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 75

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-06-26.; Abstract states three contributions: HDA/VDA framework, crossing-point asymptotics with bias-corrected Wald statistic, and workflow illustrations.; Uses synthetic data and Mexico's PROGRESA data.; Matched query is causality.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a methodological toolkit for detecting and analyzing sign changes in distributional treatment effects using treatment effects curves, causal forests, and crossing-point inference.

Problem: It studies distributional treatment effects, specifically points where marginal distributional effects change sign and therefore indicate treatment-effect discontinuities across the outcome distribution.

Method: The proposed framework combines Horizontal Discontinuity Analysis, which compares groups in regions with opposite-signed effects using causal forests, and Vertical Discontinuity Analysis, which studies sign-switch points where a Treatment Effects Curve crosses zero. It also adapts crossing-point asymptotics and uses a bias-corrected Wald statistic to test non-tangentiality of the local slope.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is a unified toolkit for treatment-effect discontinuities that links TEC zero-crossing inference with horizontal group comparisons via causal forests and vertical analysis of sign-switch points.

Main result: The abstract reports illustrations on synthetic data and a diagnostic application to Mexico's PROGRESA data, but does not provide quantitative performance results, effect sizes, or comparison metrics.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code, data availability, baselines, ablations, robustness checks, numerical empirical results, affiliations, DOI, or peer-review status beyond arXiv v1.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the causality feed because it develops tools for causal treatment-effect analysis, especially heterogeneous and distributional treatment effects.

Tags: causality, distributional treatment effects, heterogeneous treatment effects, causal forests, econometrics, crossing-point inference

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or schematic showing TEC estimation, zero-crossing detection, HDA regions with opposite-signed effects, and VDA testing at sign-switch points.

item.title: The Weakest Link Tells It All: Outcome-Supervised Process Reward Modeling via Learnable Credit Assignment
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 40, Paper 45, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 58, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI, journal, or conference venue, numeric empirical results, dataset and task names, baseline list details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-26.; Proposes Learnable Credit Assignment for outcome-supervised PRMs.; Frames the method as multiple instance learning with Softmax-Weighted-Sum pooling.; Claims Bayes consistency under mild assumptions.

TL;DR: The paper proposes LCA, an outcome-supervised process reward modeling framework that learns step-level credit assignment from final-answer correctness using a weakest-link multiple-instance-learning formulation.

Problem: Training process reward models for LLM reasoning usually needs costly stepwise annotations; outcome-supervised alternatives avoid this cost but struggle with credit assignment, namely identifying which reasoning steps caused a correct or incorrect final answer.

Method: LCA jointly learns credit assignment and process reward modeling from outcome labels only. It formalizes outcome-supervised PRM training as a multiple instance learning problem and introduces Softmax-Weighted-Sum pooling to model strong dependence and redundancy among reasoning states under a Weakest Link Assignment principle.

Key contribution: The main novelty is replacing uniform or causal credit assignment heuristics with a learnable weakest-link credit assignment mechanism anchored in step correctness, plus a Bayes-consistency claim for the proposed algorithm.

Main result: The abstract reports that LCA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art outcome-supervised PRMs across multiple tasks and model backbones, but it does not provide numeric gains, task names, or metric details.

Limitation: The abstract does not report datasets, exact metrics, ablations, robustness tests, baseline list, or quantitative effect sizes; code is listed only as an anonymous repository.

Relevance: This matched the query "causality" because it addresses credit assignment in reasoning chains and explicitly contrasts against causal assignment methods, though its primary contribution is process reward modeling rather than causal inference.

Tags: process reward modeling, LLM reasoning, credit assignment, multiple instance learning, outcome supervision, causality-adjacent

Figure note: A useful image would show the LCA pipeline: reasoning chain states, outcome-only supervision, learnable weakest-link credit assignment, SWS pooling, and PRM reward outputs.

item.title: PhysiFormer: Learning to Simulate Mechanics in World Space
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 26, Paper 35, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 75, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation count, h-index or prior-author impact metadata, DOI, journal or conference venue, exact quantitative results

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-06-25.; Abstract states code and models are available at the project page.; Trained on over 100k simulated trajectories.; Claims substantial improvement over autoregressive baselines in trajectory accuracy, rigidity preservation, and momentum-based consistency.

TL;DR: PhysiFormer frames physically plausible 3D object motion prediction as coordinate-space diffusion over mesh vertex trajectories, avoiding explicit rigidity or causality constraints while modeling uncertainty over future dynamics.

Problem: The paper studies how to predict physically plausible future motion for rigid and elastic 3D objects in a view-invariant world-coordinate representation rather than view-dependent video pixels or hand-designed physics latents.

Method: It proposes PhysiFormer, a diffusion transformer that takes initial mesh vertex positions, velocities, and material type, then samples future vertex trajectories through a single denoising diffusion process in world coordinates. Attention is factorized over time, space, and objects for efficient multi-object reasoning.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is showing that direct world-coordinate diffusion over vertex trajectories can learn rigid and elastic mechanics without explicit rigidity, causality, or other strong physics inductive biases, while still supporting diverse plausible futures under uncertainty.

Main result: According to the abstract, PhysiFormer is trained on over 100k simulated trajectories, generalizes to mixed-material settings, unseen real-world geometries, and larger object counts, and substantially outperforms autoregressive baselines in trajectory accuracy, rigidity preservation, and momentum-based physical consistency.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative metrics, dataset details, ablations, robustness analysis, exact baseline names, or evidence about real-world deployment beyond generalization to unseen real-world geometries.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query "causality" because the paper explicitly compares against neural physics methods that enforce causality, but its main contribution is a diffusion-based mechanics simulator that works without explicit causal inductive biases rather than a causality-focused method.

Tags: 3D motion, diffusion transformer, world models, neural physics, mesh simulation, causality, robotics, computer vision

Figure note: A useful image would be the model or pipeline figure showing mesh inputs, coordinate-space diffusion over vertex trajectories, factorized attention over time/space/objects, and sampled future 3D motion outputs.

TL;DR: The paper studies whether sparse-autoencoder features inside LLMs can be steered to reduce look-ahead bias in forecasting tasks and improve historically grounded reasoning.

Problem: LLMs used for forecasting may rely on future-leaking or time-specific knowledge rather than generalizable historical patterns, producing look-ahead-biased forecasts.

Method: The authors apply LLMs to multiple forecasting tasks, inspect internal states with sparse autoencoders, identify temporal reasoning features, and intervene by steering those features in a separate domain.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is an interpretable feature-steering approach that amplifies time-awareness features to causally shift LLM forecasting behavior away from look-ahead bias while preserving general reasoning performance.

Main result: According to the abstract, amplifying time-awareness features substantially reduces look-ahead bias on forecasting prompts while preserving general reasoning performance; steering candidate look-ahead-bias features did not produce an effect.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify models, datasets, metrics, baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, code availability, or quantitative effect sizes, so the strength and reproducibility of the result are unclear.

Relevance: This matches the query 'causality' because it uses interventions on interpretable LLM features and claims a causal shift in model behavior, though the application domain is forecasting rather than causal inference broadly.

item.title: OpenRCA 2.0: From Outcome Labels to Causal Process Supervision
item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-06-25, with comment 'work in progress.'; Introduces PAVE and OpenRCA 2.0 with 500 instances.; Evaluates 11 frontier LLMs.; Reports exact root-cause recovery of 20.7%, partial service identification of 76.0%, and grounded causal-path success of 61.5%.

TL;DR: OpenRCA 2.0 proposes PAVE, a step-wise causal process supervision protocol for root cause analysis benchmarks, showing that frontier LLM agents often identify plausible root causes without grounding them in verified causal propagation paths.

Problem: The paper studies root cause analysis evaluation for LLM agents, arguing that existing datasets label only final root causes and therefore can reward pattern matching rather than causal reasoning from fault to symptom.

Method: It introduces PAVE, a step-wise labeling protocol that uses known fault-injection interventions to reconstruct causal propagation paths via forward verification from cause to effect, and applies it to build OpenRCA 2.0 with 500 cross-system RCA instances.

Key contribution: The new contribution is causal process supervision for RCA: benchmarks include step-wise causal annotations connecting root causes to symptoms, not just outcome labels.

Main result: Across 11 frontier LLMs, exact root-cause set recovery averages 20.7%; agents identify at least one correct root-cause service in 76.0% of cases but ground it in a verified causal path only 61.5% of the time.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code/data availability, system details, baselines beyond the 11 LLM comparison, ablations, statistical robustness, or whether the 500-instance benchmark is publicly released; the arXiv comment also says 'work in progress.'

Relevance: The paper directly matches the 'causality' feed because it centers on causal propagation paths, causal annotations, and forward verification for evaluating agentic RCA rather than outcome-only diagnosis.

Tags: causality, root-cause-analysis, LLM-agents, benchmark, process-supervision, fault-injection

Figure note: A useful figure would show the PAVE pipeline: injected fault intervention, forward causal propagation verification, step-wise annotation, and comparison against outcome-only RCA labels.

item.description:

TL;DR: The paper proposes CHAUN, a cross-head attention uplift model, plus RA-IPS, an adversarial inverse-propensity weighting method intended to make uplift modeling more robust under unobserved confounding.

Problem: It studies individual treatment effect estimation for uplift modeling, focusing on two challenges: modeling similarity and differences between treatment/control groups, and reducing bias when unobserved confounding affects propensity estimates.

Method: CHAUN uses shared feature embeddings with cross-head attention to dynamically combine treatment-specific and control-specific representations. RA-IPS adversarially optimizes propensity weights inside constrained uncertainty sets when true propensity scores are unavailable.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is the combination of cross-head attention for inter-group representation sharing in uplift modeling with a robust adversarial IPS scheme for unobserved confounding, supported by an identifiability result when true propensity scores are available.

Main result: Across CRITEO-UPLIFT, LAZADA, and a production e-commerce dataset, the abstract reports up to 25.6% relative QINI-score improvement over state-of-the-art uplift models; RA-IPS reportedly improves robustness over standard IPS by 5.4% under unobserved confounding.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention code release, affiliation, DOI, peer-review venue, ablations, statistical significance, uncertainty-set design details, or how unobserved confounding is simulated/measured, so reproducibility and robustness evidence remain unclear.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it targets ITE/uplift modeling, propensity-score weighting, identifiability, and robustness to unobserved confounding.

Tags: causal inference, uplift modeling, individual treatment effects, inverse propensity score, unobserved confounding, attention networks, robustness

item.title: Inherited Circuits, Learned Semantics: How Fine-Tuning Creates Evasion Vulnerabilities Invisible to Standard Evaluation
item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-25 with no DOI, journal reference, or conference metadata supplied.; Abstract explicitly studies Foundation-Sec-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on matched PowerShell classification cohorts.; Abstract reports causal interventions localizing the classification circuit to a late-attention route inherited from Llama.; Abstract describes a three-tier evasion benchmark and specific missed transformations: iwr substitution, Invoke-Expression reconstruction, and case mutation.

TL;DR: The paper argues that security fine-tuning can preserve standard held-out accuracy while creating transformation-sensitive evasion failures, and uses causal circuit analysis plus probing to identify brittle indicator-token semantics in PowerShell classifiers.

Problem: Standard evaluation of fine-tuned LLM security classifiers on same-distribution held-out data can miss vulnerabilities that appear under behavior-preserving command transformations.

Method: The paper compares Foundation-Sec-8B-Instruct with its base Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on matched PowerShell classification cohorts, applies causal interventions to localize a classification circuit, evaluates a three-tier evasion benchmark, and proposes pre-deployment monitoring via a linear probe plus an indicator-token sign test.

Key contribution: It connects fine-tuning-induced evasion vulnerability to inherited model circuits whose semantics become concentrated and specialized after fine-tuning, rather than treating failures as ordinary distribution shift alone.

Main result: From the abstract, Foundation-Sec improves baseline behavior but misses transformed variants involving iwr substitution, Invoke-Expression reconstruction, and case-mutated Invoke-Expression/IEX cases that the base Llama model does not share.

Limitation: The abstract does not report quantitative accuracy, evasion rates, dataset sizes, statistical tests, code availability, or whether results generalize beyond PowerShell and the two studied 8B models.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the paper uses causal interventions to localize classification circuits and studies how fine-tuning changes causal/semantic behavior in security classifiers.

item.title: Where Do Models Find Happiness? Emotion Vectors in Open-Source LLMs
item.description:
Priority: 53/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 30, Paper 35, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 75, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference venue supplied., No repository URL or repository-quality metadata supplied., No dataset size or public data URL supplied., No ablation details supplied in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-25 in cs.CL and cs.AI.; Studies Apertus-8B-Instruct-2509 and Gemma-4-E4B-it.; Reports peak PC1-valence correlations of r = 0.76 and r = 0.83.; Reports corpus-sensitive arousal alignment, with r up to 0.45 for Gemma-generated stories and r <= 0.21 for Apertus-generated stories.

TL;DR: The paper tests whether previously reported emotion-vector phenomena generalize from Claude Sonnet 4.5 to two open-weight instruction-tuned LLMs and finds strong but architecture- and corpus-dependent valence/arousal structure.

Problem: The paper studies whether internal emotion representations, especially valence and arousal geometry, are a general property of language models rather than a finding specific to Claude Sonnet 4.5.

Method: It extracts emotion contrast vectors across all layers of Apertus-8B-Instruct-2509 and Gemma-4-E4B-it using two model-generated corpora, then analyzes principal-component alignment with psychological dimensions such as valence and arousal.

Key contribution: Beyond replication, the novel contribution is a cross-model, layerwise comparison showing that valence representations emerge at different depths in different open-weight models, and that arousal alignment depends strongly on the corpus used for extraction.

Main result: Both models recover valence geometry, with peak PC1-valence correlations of r = 0.76 and r = 0.83, comparable to the r = 0.81 reported for Claude; arousal alignment is weaker and corpus-sensitive, reaching r up to 0.45 with Gemma-generated stories but no more than 0.21 with Apertus-generated stories.

Limitation: The abstract does not report causal intervention results for the open-weight models, statistical uncertainty, ablations, full baseline details, dataset size, or robustness checks beyond the two models and two generated corpora.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because it builds on prior work claiming emotion vectors causally influence model behavior, though the supplied abstract mainly supports representational replication and geometry analysis rather than new causal evidence.

Tags: LLM interpretability, emotion vectors, representation geometry, mechanistic interpretability, open-weight models, valence, arousal, causality-adjacent

Method: The authors derive the semiparametric influence function for HTC estimators across all HTC-identified directed mixed graphs, with recursive corrections for uncertainty from earlier estimation stages, then use it to obtain asymptotic normality and closed-form variance formulas.

Main result: The abstract claims the HTC estimator is asymptotically normal with closed-form computable variance, and demonstrates the method on the Fulton Fish Market dataset to summarize the causal effect of supply on demand.

Limitation: The abstract reports one applied dataset but does not mention simulations, finite-sample performance, robustness checks, software, code, or comparisons against alternative inference methods.

item.description:

TL;DR: TAVR-VLM proposes a risk-conditioned causal grounding framework for TAVR report generation that aims to reduce anatomically ungrounded hallucinations in multimodal medical LLM outputs.

Problem: The paper studies hallucination in multimodal large language models for high-stakes Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement planning, where generated reports may lack anatomical grounding despite requiring multimodal clinical reasoning.

Method: It introduces TAVR-VLM with Risk-Conditioned Causal Grounding Attention, which forms a model-internal Risk -> Region -> Word pathway: multimodal inputs are compressed through a causal risk bottleneck into a global risk mask, and autoregressive generation is constrained by a support-projected causal consistency objective at token level.

Key contribution: The novel element is the explicit risk-conditioned causal grounding mechanism that links predicted clinical risk to visual regions and generated words, rather than relying on generic multimodal attention or post-hoc grounding.

Main result: From the abstract, TAVR-VLM is evaluated on M3TAVR, a 1,482-patient cohort, and reports state-of-the-art performance with AUROC 0.896, CIDEr 0.936, and hallucination rate reduced to 8.1%.

Limitation: The abstract does not report baselines, ablations, confidence intervals, external validation, code availability, data accessibility, or details on how hallucination rate was measured.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the causality query because its central mechanism is framed as causal grounding, including a causal risk bottleneck and causal consistency objective for multimodal generation.

item.description:

TL;DR: The paper studies budget-aware experimental design for instrumental-variable causal effect estimation when partial measurements can be collected more cheaply than complete samples.

Problem: It addresses how to improve estimation accuracy for a causal effect in instrumental variable regression when some prior joint-distribution information is available but additional data collection is costly and can include partial measurements of only some variables.

Method: The paper develops an adaptive design framework for a Gaussian graphical model, assigning costs to complete and partial samples and analytically optimizing the number of each sample type under a budget constraint. It also derives significance, power, and sample-size calculations for detecting a non-zero causal effect under the optimized allocation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, relative to standard IV estimation with uniformly complete samples, is the explicit use of costed partial measurements to reduce asymptotic estimator variance and budget requirements under specific Gaussian parameter configurations.

Main result: The abstract claims that partial samples such as measurements of X_12 can reduce asymptotic variance and that the proposed allocation can considerably reduce the required budget and number of complete samples, but it does not report concrete numerical effect sizes.

Limitation: The abstract restricts the main theoretical claim to specific parameter configurations in a Gaussian graphical model and does not provide code, datasets, quantitative benchmark results, robustness checks, or details on how broad the practical gains are.

Relevance: This directly matches the causal inference feed because it proposes an experimental design method for estimating causal effects with instrumental variables, with applications framed around adaptive causal effect estimation.

Tags: causal inference, instrumental variables, experimental design, partial measurements, Gaussian graphical models, budget allocation, sample-size planning

Figure note: A useful image would show the Gaussian graphical model/instrumental-variable setup and the budget-allocation pipeline comparing complete and partial sampling strategies.

TL;DR: LiveEdit proposes a diffusion-based framework for causal, frame-by-frame streaming video editing that preserves unedited content while reaching real-time-ish inference speed.

Problem: The paper studies practical streaming video editing, where edited regions must be controlled while backgrounds and non-edited regions remain temporally stable, and where latency must be low enough for interactive or AR use.

Method: It introduces a three-stage distillation pipeline that transfers editing capability from a bidirectional foundation model to an efficient unidirectional streaming editor, plus an AR-oriented mask cache that reuses region-related computation across frames.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is combining causal unidirectional video editing with progressive distillation from a stronger bidirectional model and a mask-cache mechanism tailored to region-specific streaming edits.

Main result: The abstract reports state-of-the-art visual quality among streaming baselines and inference speed of 12.66 FPS on a dedicated streaming video editing benchmark.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, model size, hardware, ablations, robustness tests, user studies, code release, or whether 12.66 FPS holds across resolutions and edit types.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query because it explicitly performs causal frame-by-frame editing with a unidirectional streaming editor, though the use of 'causal' appears to mean temporally causal generation rather than causal inference.

Tags: streaming-video-editing, diffusion-models, causal-generation, real-time-video, distillation, augmented-reality

item.title: Socratic agents for autonomous scientific discovery in high-dimensional physical systems
item.description:
Priority: 57/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 34, Paper 55, Empirical 82, Reproducibility 38, Manuscript 82

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-25 in cs.AI and physics.optics.; Abstract describes AHOIS as a multi-agent AI scientist with a physics-critic agent.; Evaluation uses a real multimode-fibre optical platform rather than only simulation.; Reported metrics include effective rank 56.9 and classification accuracies of 76.97% on MNIST and 83.17% on Fashion-MNIST.

TL;DR: AHOIS is a multi-agent AI scientist that uses Socratic causal critique to autonomously form, test, and revise physical hypotheses in a real high-dimensional multimode-fibre optical system.

Problem: The paper studies how to move autonomous scientific discovery systems beyond fixed human-designed workflows toward epistemic autonomy: constructing, challenging, falsifying, and revising physical explanations from experimental evidence.

Method: It proposes AHOIS, a closed-loop multi-agent framework with a physics-critic agent that performs causal questioning, constraint checking, counterexample generation, and falsification-criteria formulation during experimentation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is the embedding of Socratic hypothesis interrogation into autonomous experimentation, allowing the system to self-correct physical explanations rather than merely optimize a predefined experimental workflow.

Main result: On a real multimode-fibre optical platform, AHOIS discovered a random-interference encoding hypothesis, sparse-measurement strategies, and failure modes; the discovered encoding achieved effective rank 56.9 for 16x16 measurements and classification accuracies of 76.97% on MNIST and 83.17% on Fashion-MNIST. Ablations reportedly show Socratic interrogation improves physical consistency, hypothesis completeness, uncertainty calibration, and experimental-plan validity.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code release, public data, statistical uncertainty, detailed baselines against non-Socratic agents, robustness across additional physical platforms, affiliations, citation signals, DOI, or peer-reviewed venue status.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because its physics-critic agent explicitly uses causal questioning, counterexample generation, and falsification criteria to test hypotheses in an autonomous scientific discovery loop.

Tags: autonomous science, multi-agent systems, causal reasoning, scientific discovery, closed-loop experimentation, physics optics, hypothesis falsification

Figure note: A useful image would be the likely framework/pipeline figure showing AHOIS agents, the Socratic physics-critic loop, hypothesis revision, and closed-loop interaction with the multimode-fibre optical platform.

Score evidence: Accepted at ADKDD 2026 according to the comment field.; Abstract states the framework uses incrementality experiments as causal anchors.; Abstract reports forward-in-time validation against channel-level incrementality experiment readouts.; Abstract reports comparisons against raw attribution and fine-grained ML baselines.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a causally calibrated correction layer for advertising attribution that uses incrementality experiments to estimate and allocate cannibalization-adjusted daily conversion signals.

Problem: Production paid-attribution metrics can overstate true incremental growth when paid channels overlap with organic, brand-driven, or other acquisition demand, causing distorted ROI estimates and budget decisions.

Method: The authors propose an experiment-calibrated attribution correction framework that uses sparse incrementality experiments as causal anchors, converts lift measurements into daily correction estimates, and allocates calibrated cannibalization volume across business hierarchies under structural consistency constraints.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is a production-oriented bridge from sparse causal lift experiments to daily, hierarchy-consistent attribution corrections for large-scale advertising operations, rather than relying on raw attribution or fine-grained ML prediction alone.

Main result: Offline forward-in-time validation reportedly reduces calibration error versus raw attribution and fine-grained ML baselines; deployment across multiple global TikTok markets was followed by an approximately 15-percentage-point reduction in measured cannibalization rate.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide dataset details, exact metrics, confidence intervals, ablations, code/data availability, or enough information to separate the effect of the deployed system from concurrent strategy changes.

Relevance: The paper matches the causality query because it uses incrementality experiments as causal anchors for correcting observational attribution signals in advertising systems.

Tags: causal inference, incrementality experiments, advertising attribution, cannibalization, budget allocation, production ML, cs.IR, cs.LG

Figure note: A framework or pipeline figure showing the flow from raw attribution and incrementality experiments to daily correction estimates and hierarchy-consistent allocation would be useful; the metadata notes 3 figures but provides no direct image URL.

TL;DR: The paper studies weak-instrument bias in multivariable Mendelian randomization and proposes a Bayesian framework, MVMR-Pony, that the abstract reports outperforms existing frequentist approaches in simulations.

Problem: Weak instruments can bias causal inference in Mendelian randomization, and the problem is harder in multivariable Mendelian randomization because instruments that look strong marginally for one exposure may be weak after conditioning on other exposures.

Method: The paper introduces or evaluates MVMR-Pony, a Bayesian framework for mitigating weak-instrument bias in multivariable Mendelian randomization, and compares it against existing frequentist methods using simulation studies.

Key contribution: The main novelty is applying a Bayesian weak-instrument mitigation approach specifically to multivariable Mendelian randomization, where fewer methods exist than in the single-exposure setting, and evaluating it across multiple weak-instrument mechanisms.

Main result: According to the abstract, MVMR-Pony outperforms frequentist approaches on bias, coverage, type I error rates, and power in simulations involving correlated genetic effects, measurement error, and mediation.

Limitation: Evidence in the supplied abstract is limited to simulation studies; no real-data application, code availability, sensitivity analyses, affiliations, DOI, peer-review venue, or robustness details are provided in the metadata.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the causal inference feed because it targets valid causal inference under weak instruments in multivariable Mendelian randomization, a statistically important causal inference setting.

item.title: Doubly Robust Quadratic Inference Functions for Causal Inference in Cluster Randomized Trials
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 40, Paper 45, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: No DOI, journal, or conference venue provided., No affiliation metadata provided., No citation count or h-index information provided., No code repository or software package mentioned., No public data availability statement mentioned., No ablation, sensitivity, or robustness details visible in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-06-25.; Single listed author: Hengshi Yu.; Abstract states consistency if either propensity score or outcome regression model is correctly specified.; Abstract compares DR-QIF with DR-GEE and reports up to 3.5% efficiency gain at N=120 and T=8.

TL;DR: This paper proposes a doubly robust quadratic inference function estimator for average treatment effects in cluster randomized trials with covariate imbalance and correlated longitudinal outcomes.

Problem: Existing QIF methods for marginal regression with correlated data do not address confounding from covariate imbalance between treatment arms, which matters in observational CRTs and CRT analyses with prognostic covariate adjustment.

Method: The paper combines doubly robust pseudo-outcomes, built from propensity score and outcome regression models, with QIF extended score equations to estimate average treatment effects under correlated CRT data.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is a DR-QIF estimator that retains consistency if either the propensity score model or outcome regression model is correctly specified, while targeting efficiency gains over DR-GEE when the working correlation structure is misspecified.

Main result: From the abstract, DR-QIF is analytically shown to be more efficient than DR-GEE under misspecified working correlation; in cross-sectional CRTs the two are algebraically identical, while in longitudinal CRTs with strong temporal correlation the reported efficiency gain reaches 3.5% at N=120 and T=8.

Limitation: The abstract reports Monte Carlo simulation and one WASH Benefits Kenya application, but does not mention code, public implementation, robustness checks beyond the described simulations, or validation across multiple empirical datasets.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it studies doubly robust average treatment effect estimation in clustered randomized trial settings with confounding/covariate imbalance.

Tags: causal inference, doubly robust estimation, quadratic inference functions, cluster randomized trials, correlated data, marginal regression, Monte Carlo simulation

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline diagram showing how propensity score and outcome regression models form doubly robust pseudo-outcomes, which are then passed into QIF extended score equations for clustered longitudinal treatment-effect estimation.

They evaluate activation-level reconstruction and model-level recovery, then test feature intervention via refusal steering.

Key contribution: A broad SAE resource for instruction-tuned Qwen3 models, including multiple activation sites and model sizes, plus evidence that selected SAE features can causally steer refusal behavior.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-25 in cs.CE.; Abstract reports controlled ablations comparing DPO agents with identical-SFT controls at 8B and 70B.; Abstract reports concrete metrics: rho=0.70, N_eff about 1.4, 67.6% market accuracy versus 70.2% standalone agent.; Abstract reports mitigation comparison where cross-model diversity lowers rho from 0.68 to 0.40.

TL;DR: The paper argues that DPO-aligned LLM trading agents develop highly correlated forecasting errors, undermining the independence assumption behind LLM-based prediction markets.

Problem: It studies whether prediction markets still aggregate independent signals when the participants are LLM agents, especially agents fine-tuned with Direct Preference Optimization.

Method: The abstract describes controlled comparisons of DPO-tuned agents against identical-SFT controls at 8B and 70B scales, measures pairwise error correlation and effective independent forecaster count, tests scaling from 5 to 40 agents, and evaluates mitigation strategies such as cross-model diversity.

Key contribution: The novel claim is that preference optimization itself causally drives correlated errors and market monoculture, rather than the issue being solved by adding more same-family LLM agents.

Main result: DPO agents show pairwise error correlation of rho=0.70, reducing 10 agents to about 1.4 effective independent forecasters; performance of a 10-agent market is reported as 67.6%, below a single standalone agent at 70.2%, and cross-model diversity reduces rho from 0.68 to 0.40.

Limitation: The abstract does not identify datasets, market-question selection, code availability, full baselines, statistical uncertainty, affiliations, or external validation beyond the stated lab and scale replications.

Relevance: The matched query is causality: the paper explicitly claims two controlled ablations isolate preference optimization as the causal driver of correlated LLM-agent errors in prediction markets.

Tags: LLM agents, prediction markets, Direct Preference Optimization, causal ablation, forecasting, error correlation, AI alignment

Figure note: A useful figure would show the causal pipeline from DPO to convergent output distributions to correlated forecasting errors, alongside N_eff scaling curves and mitigation effects for cross-model diversity.

item.title: Perception, Verdict, and Evolution: Hindsight-Driven Self-Refining Forensics Agent for AI-Generated Image Detection
item.description:
Priority: 45/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 16, Paper 35, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI or venue acceptance, open-source code, repository quality, public release details for generated reasoning traces

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.CV and cs.AI, 10 pages, published 2026-06-25.; Reports 82.18% accuracy on Chameleon, +16.41 percentage points over AIDE.; Reports 93.3% mean accuracy on AIGCDetect-Benchmark across 16 generators.; Claims external evaluation against GPT-5 and GPT-5-mini for consistency and causal grounding.

TL;DR: ForeAgent is an agentic multimodal forensics framework that detects AI-generated images by combining multi-view forensic cues with MLLM-based verdict reasoning and iterative hindsight-driven self-refinement.

Problem: The paper studies AI-generated image and deepfake detection under rapidly improving generative models, where existing detectors may miss fine-grained forensic artifacts and depend on costly, static synthetic supervision from frontier models.

Method: It proposes ForeAgent, using a Perception-Verdict architecture that aggregates semantic, spatial, and frequency-domain cues, then uses an MLLM verdict module to fuse them into a reasoning-backed decision. It further introduces a Hindsight-Driven Self-Refining strategy with Sampling-Reflection-Evolution: inference rollouts are checked against labels, failed or weak reasoning traces are regenerated, filtered by a dual-expert quality gate, and used for continual fine-tuning.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element appears to be the combination of multi-view forensic perception, MLLM verdict reasoning, and iterative self-curation/self-evolution of reasoning traces for AI-image forensics, rather than relying only on static detector training or fixed synthetic supervision.

Main result: From the abstract, ForeAgent reports state-of-the-art performance on Chameleon with 82.18% accuracy, a +16.41 percentage point gain over AIDE, and 93.3% mean accuracy on AIGCDetect-Benchmark across 16 generators; external evaluation claims more consistent and causally grounded reasoning than GPT-5 and GPT-5-mini.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide code availability, data release, ablation details, robustness tests, statistical significance, model size/cost, or enough information to verify whether the causal-grounding claim is rigorously measured.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it explicitly claims more causally grounded reasoning for AI-generated image detection, and its agentic reflection/evolution mechanism may be relevant to researchers tracking causal reasoning in multimodal detection systems.

Tags: AI-generated image detection, deepfake detection, MLLM, agentic framework, self-refinement, forensics, causal reasoning

item.title: A Unified Three-Stage Weighting Framework for Causal Inference and Mediation Analysis under Case-Control Sampling
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 45, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations or prior topic record, citation count or h-index, DOI, journal, or conference venue, open-source code, public implementation details beyond abstract, dataset access details for the application

Score evidence: arXiv v1 posted 2026-06-25 in stat.ME.; Abstract states a unified three-stage weighting framework for causal inference and mediation analysis from case-control studies.; Method combines density-ratio learning, label-shift correction, prevalence-based design weights, and marginal structural modeling.; Empirical evidence mentioned includes simulation studies and an NHANES application.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a three-stage weighting framework to estimate causal and mediation effects from case-control studies despite retrospective, outcome-dependent sampling.

Problem: Case-control sampling improves efficiency for rare outcomes but distorts the population outcome distribution, making standard causal inference and causal mediation analyses biased when they ignore the sampling design.

Method: The proposed 3S-weighting framework first estimates population outcome prevalence using density-ratio learning, label-shift correction, and external covariate information; then uses prevalence-based design weights to reconstruct the target population; and finally applies stabilized causal and mediation weights in marginal structural models to estimate total and pathway-specific effects.

Key contribution: The novelty is a unified weighting pipeline that links prevalence recovery, case-control design correction, and marginal structural causal/mediation weighting, including decomposition into pure direct, pure indirect, and interaction effects.

Main result: The abstract reports simulations where conventional analyses that ignore retrospective sampling show substantial bias, while the proposed method consistently recovers target population causal parameters across sampling scenarios; an NHANES application illustrates implementation.

Limitation: The abstract does not report numerical effect sizes, comparison against multiple causal-inference baselines, ablations, robustness checks, code availability, or detailed assumptions required for density-ratio/label-shift correction.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the 'causal inference' feed because it directly studies causal effect and mediation estimation under a nonstandard but common epidemiologic sampling design.

Tags: causal inference, causal mediation, case-control studies, weighting, marginal structural models, density-ratio learning, label shift, epidemiology

Figure note: A pipeline figure showing the three stages, from prevalence estimation through target-population reconstruction to causal/mediation weighting, would be useful; the metadata says the paper has 3 figures but provides no direct image URL.

item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv version is v1, published 2026-06-25.; The abstract states the model uses a frozen AR context tower and a trainable diffusion denoiser tower.; The model is built on Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B and trained on approximately 2.1T tokens.; The abstract reports 98.7% AR-baseline quality retention and 2.42x higher wall-clock generation throughput.

TL;DR: Nemotron-TwoTower proposes a two-tower diffusion language model that separates autoregressive context encoding from block-wise diffusion denoising, aiming to improve generation throughput while preserving most AR baseline quality.

Problem: The paper studies a limitation of diffusion language models: using a single network for both causal context representation and iterative denoising may constrain capacity and reduce effectiveness for both roles.

Method: It proposes TwoTower, a block-wise autoregressive diffusion framework with a frozen autoregressive context tower for clean-token causal processing and a trainable diffusion denoiser tower with bidirectional block attention, connected through cross-attention to the context.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element described in the abstract is the architectural decoupling of pretrained AR context processing from diffusion denoising, implemented on top of Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B rather than training one shared network to do both jobs.

Main result: According to the abstract, Nemotron-TwoTower is trained on about 2.1T tokens, retains 98.7% of the autoregressive baseline's quality, and achieves 2.42x higher wall-clock generation throughput.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify benchmark names, metric definitions, ablations, robustness tests, statistical uncertainty, or detailed comparisons against other diffusion language models.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it describes causal processing in the autoregressive context tower, but it appears to be about causal attention/generation order in language modeling rather than causality or causal inference as a scientific topic.

item.title: Epiphany-Aware KV Cache Eviction Without the Attention Matrix
item.description:
Priority: 41/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 14, Paper 35, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 65

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation count and h-index not supplied., No DOI, venue, or acceptance signal supplied., No code repository or data release mentioned., No ablations, robustness tests, statistical uncertainty, or limitation discussion visible in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 preprint marked 'in review'; no DOI or journal reference provided.; Method claims no training, classifier, custom kernel, or attention matrix.; Reports MATH-500, AIME-2024, baseline comparisons against ThinKV and H2O, and up to 2.8x speed.; Matched query is 'causality', but the abstract only shows a 'causal rolling z-score' mechanism.

TL;DR: EpiKV proposes a training-free KV cache eviction method for long reasoning traces that scores token importance from representation changes instead of attention weights, enabling FlashAttention-compatible inference with reported accuracy and speed gains under fixed cache budgets.

Problem: The paper studies KV cache growth as a deployment bottleneck for reasoning models that generate very long chains of thought, arguing that attention-weight-based eviction is noisy and incompatible with fused attention kernels because it requires materializing attention matrices.

Method: It introduces an epiphany score based on changes in the model's internal representation observed during the forward pass, then uses this score for cache eviction without training, classifiers, attention-matrix materialization, custom kernels, or changes to FlashAttention stacks. The abstract also mentions layer-sign handling and a causal rolling z-score to remove positional trends, but the exact algorithmic details are only partially specified.

Key contribution: The main novelty is replacing attention-weight importance proxies with a forward-pass representation-change metric for KV eviction, allowing production-style fused-kernel inference while reportedly scaling feasible context length up to 16x beyond attention-based scoring methods.

Main result: At a 4096-token cache, EpiKV reports 72% on MATH-500, comparable to ThinKV at 71% and above H2O at 67%; a lag-normalized variant reports 37% on AIME-2024 at 8192 tokens versus 33% for the best compared baseline, with up to 2.8x speedup.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention code release, public implementation details, ablations, robustness tests, statistical uncertainty, broader datasets beyond MATH-500 and AIME-2024, or independent validation. The causality relevance appears weak: the match likely comes from 'causal rolling z-score' rather than causal inference or causal modeling.

Relevance: Relevant mainly if the researcher tracks long-context LLM inference, KV cache compression, or reasoning-model deployment. For the configured query 'causality', relevance is low because the paper appears to use 'causal' in the temporal/online-normalization sense, not as a causality research contribution.

Tags: KV-cache, LLM-inference, long-context, reasoning-models, FlashAttention, cache-eviction, weak-causality-match

Figure note: A useful figure would show the EpiKV inference pipeline: representation-change scoring during the forward pass, causal rolling z-score normalization, token ranking, and KV eviction inside a FlashAttention-compatible stack.
